stem: A 52-year-old man is brought to the clinic by his wife because of fatigue, decreased appetite, and intermittent abdominal discomfort over the past several months. The wife has noticed yellowing of his eyes and increased irritability. He has also complained of diffuse muscle weakness and occasional nausea, but no vomiting or diarrhea. He denies abdominal trauma or recent travel. Over the past year, he has lost about 6 kg (13 lb) without trying. The patient works as a construction foreman and says his job is stressful, often leading him to have “a few beers” after work. On further questioning, he admits to drinking 8–10 beers daily for more than 25 years, with occasional whiskey on weekends. He smokes one pack of cigarettes per day and has done so for 30 years. His diet consists mainly of fast food and processed snacks, and he rarely exercises. He has never been hospitalized and has no history of blood transfusions or intravenous drug use. Review of systems is notable for mild difficulty sleeping, decreased libido, and occasional easy bruising. There is no history of hematemesis, melena, or hematochezia. He denies chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath. Family history is notable for his father’s death from myocardial infarction at age 63 and his mother’s death from breast cancer at age 70. On physical examination, the patient appears tired but alert. Vital signs: temperature 37.0°C (98.6°F), blood pressure 138/84 mm Hg, pulse 88/min, respirations 18/min. BMI is 27 kg/m². There is mild scleral icterus, palmar erythema, and multiple spider angiomata over the upper chest and shoulders. The abdomen is soft but mildly distended, with a liver edge palpable 3 cm below the right costal margin; the liver is firm and mildly tender. Shifting dullness is absent. There is no splenomegaly. No peripheral edema is noted. Laboratory studies show: AST 178 U/L ALT 74 U/L ALP 250 IU/L GGT Elevated Total bilirubin 3.1 mg/d L Albumin Mildly decreased INR Mildly prolonged Hepatitis B and C serologies are negative. Abdominal ultrasound shows an enlarged liver with heterogeneous echotexture, no focal masses, and patent hepatic vessels. A liver biopsy is performed to evaluate the degree of inflammation and fibrosis. Histopathology demonstrates swollen hepatocytes with ballooning degeneration, infiltration by neutrophils, and irregular, rope-like eosinophilic inclusions in the cytoplasm. Which of the following histologic findings is most characteristic of this patient’s condition?
explanation: Mallory hyaline (aka Mallory–Denk bodies) are irregular, eosinophilic, rope-like cytoplasmic inclusions found in hepatocytes. They are composed of damaged intermediate filaments, primarily cytokeratins 8 and 18, that have undergone cytoskeletal disruption due to oxidative stress and toxic injury. In alcoholic hepatitis, chronic ethanol intake leads to excess NADH production via alcohol dehydrogenase and aldehyde dehydrogenase activity, impairing fatty acid oxidation and promoting triglyceride accumulation. In addition, acetaldehyde (the toxic metabolite of ethanol) forms adducts with proteins, disrupts microtubule function, and damages the cytoskeleton, resulting in the aggregation of intermediate filaments into Mallory bodies. stem: A 52-year-old man is brought to the emergency department with high-grade fever, abdominal discomfort, and progressive confusion for one day. His family reports he has a history of alcohol use and was recently treated for hepatic encephalopathy. On examination, he appears lethargic. Temperature is 39.2°C (102.6°F), blood pressure is 90/60 mm Hg, heart rate is 115/min, respiratory rate is 22/min, and oxygen saturation is 96% on room air. His abdomen is distended with shifting dullness and mild diffuse tenderness, but no rebound. Laboratory studies reveal WBC 14,500/µL, serum creatinine 1.6 mg/d L, and total bilirubin 3.2 mg/d L. Diagnostic paracentesis shows a neutrophil count of 430 cells/mm³ and total protein 0.8 g/d L. Cultures are pending. What is the most likely diagnosis? Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) is a life-threatening infection in patients with ascites, often due to cirrhosis. It presents with fever, abdominal pain, and an elevated neutrophil count in ascitic fluid (≥250 cells/mm³). Prompt diagnosis via paracentesis and early treatment with third-generation cephalosporins are essential to reduce morbidity and mortality.
explanation: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) is the most likely diagnosis in this patient who presents with fever, abdominal distension, and ascitic fluid showing elevated neutrophils. SBP is a peritoneal infection that arises in patients with ascites, typically due to cirrhosis, without a clear intraabdominal source of infection. The condition frequently develops through bacterial translocation from the gut, facilitated by portal hypertension. This occurs via compromised intestinal barrier integrity, increased gut permeability, and reduced host immunity. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.

stem: A 62-year-old man with a history of chronic hepatitis B infection presents to the clinic for evaluation of unintentional weight loss and early satiety over the past few months. He has also noticed vague right upper quadrant discomfort that has slowly worsened. He denies fever, jaundice, or change in bowel habits. His last visit was over a year ago, and he has not undergone any recent imaging. On exam, he has mild hepatomegaly without tenderness. Laboratory testing reveals a mild normocytic anemia and elevated alkaline phosphatase. Liver function tests are otherwise within normal limits. A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the abdomen is obtained and shows a single, large, heterogeneous mass in the right lobe of the liver. The liver findings in this patient are similar to those shown in the representative gross pathology specimen below. He has never had a colonoscopy and has no personal or family history of colorectal cancer. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This patient presents with constitutional symptoms (weight loss, early satiety), vague right upper quadrant discomfort, chronic hepatitis B infection, and imaging that reveals a large, solitary, heterogeneous liver mass — classic findings of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). Chronic hepatitis B is a major risk factor for HCC, and patients may develop malignancy even in the absence of cirrhosis. His physical exam reveals hepatomegaly without tenderness, and labs show elevated alkaline phosphatase, which may reflect intrahepatic bile duct compression by the growing tumor. The contrast-enhanced CT scan showing a solitary, heterogeneous liver mass is highly suggestive of HCC in the setting of hepatitis B. stem: A 6-day-old term newborn boy is brought to the emergency department because of increasing lethargy and poor feeding. He was born at 39 weeks via spontaneous vaginal delivery after an uncomplicated pregnancy. No resuscitation was required, and Apgar scores were 8 and 9 at 1 and 5 minutes. The infant was discharged home at 48 hours of life. Parents report that mild yellowing of the skin was first noticed within the first 24 hours after birth and has progressively worsened. Over the past two days, the infant has become more difficult to wake for feeds, and when he does latch, he often sucks weakly and quickly falls asleep. They also describe a new intermittent high-pitched cry and occasional arching of the back. On exam, the infant is somnolent but arousable with vigorous stimulation. He has diffuse jaundice involving the face, trunk, and lower extremities, along with scleral icterus. Muscle tone is decreased overall, but on startling he briefly stiffens and extends the limbs before relaxing. The anterior fontanelle is flat, and no hepatosplenomegaly is present. Vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory studies reveal: hematocrit 52% (normal for neonates), total bilirubin 29.8 mg/d L, direct bilirubin 1.3 mg/d L. The infant had previously received phototherapy at a community clinic, but bilirubin levels continued to rise. Which of the following brain regions is most characteristically affected in this infant’s current condition? Kernicterus occurs when unconjugated bilirubin crosses the immature blood–brain barrier and deposits in basal ganglia grey matter. The putamen (along with globus pallidus and brainstem nuclei) is a classic site of bilirubin deposition. Early signs: poor feeding, lethargy, high-pitched cry, hypotonia/posturing → late sequelae: choreoathetoid CP, hearing loss, upward gaze palsy.
explanation: This infant presents with kernicterus (bilirubin encephalopathy), a neurologic condition caused by deposition of unconjugated bilirubin in specific regions of the central nervous system. The clinical picture strongly supports this diagnosis: persistent jaundice beginning in the first 24 hours of life (pathologic jaundice), extreme unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia with total bilirubin nearing 30 mg/d L, and neurologic findings including lethargy, poor feeding, hypotonia alternating with stiffness, high-pitched cry, and abnormal posturing. These features represent early manifestations of bilirubin neurotoxicity. The hallmark pathologic substrate of kernicterus is deposition of bilirubin in grey matter nuclei, particularly in the basal ganglia. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: This infant presents with kernicterus (bilirubin encephalopathy), a neurologic condition caused by deposition of unconjugated bilirubin in specific regions of the central nervous system. The clinical picture strongly supports this diagnosis: persistent jaundice beginning in the.

stem: A 59-year-old man comes to the clinic because of intermittent streaks of bright red blood noted on the toilet paper and occasionally dripping into the bowl after bowel movements. Over the past 8 months, he has noticed a feeling of fullness in the rectum but no significant pain. He denies weight loss, melena, abdominal pain, or family history of colorectal cancer. He has a long-standing history of constipation and often strains to pass hard stools. His medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension, both controlled with medication. He does not drink alcohol or smoke. On examination, his abdomen is soft and nontender. Rectal exam does not reveal any tender nodules, but anoscopy shows engorged vascular cushions protruding into the lumen above the level of the dentate line that do not cause pain when touched. Which of the following veins is the primary route of venous drainage from the site of this patient’s lesion?
explanation: This patient’s painless bright red bleeding, sense of rectal fullness, and non-tender vascular cushions seen above the dentate (pectinate) line on anoscopy are classic for internal hemorrhoids. Above the dentate line, the mucosa is innervated by visceral afferents (no sharp somatic pain), explaining why internal hemorrhoids often bleed without significant pain. The venous plexus here drains via the superior rectal vein → inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) → portal system, making the inferior mesenteric vein the primary outflow from the site of this lesion. Clinically relevant anorectal anatomy hinges on the dentate line. stem: A 9-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his parents due to poor weight gain, bloating, and chronic loose stools over the past 8 months. His parents report that he was previously growing well but has fallen behind his peers over the past year. He has no history of fever or vomiting and eats a varied diet, though his symptoms tend to worsen after meals. The patient has also developed increased irritability, occasional abdominal cramping, and persistent fatigue. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he is up to date on vaccinations. Physical examination reveals a thin boy with a mildly distended abdomen, pallor, and flat buttocks. Growth chart review shows a drop from the 60th to the 15th percentile for weight over the past year. Laboratory studies reveal hemoglobin of 10.1 g/d L, mean corpuscular volume of 72 f L, and a low serum iron concentration. Anti-tissue transglutaminase Ig A antibody is elevated. A small bowel biopsy is performed. Which of the following is the most likely histologic finding? Celiac disease is characterized by small bowel villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, and intraepithelial lymphocytosis. Diagnosis is confirmed with positive serology and small bowel biopsy.

options.C: Hypertrophied gastric rugae with protein loss

explanation: This patient presents with classic features of celiac disease, a chronic autoimmune enteropathy triggered by exposure to dietary gluten in genetically susceptible individuals. The hallmark histologic feature is villous atrophy of the small bowel mucosa, accompanied by crypt hyperplasia and intraepithelial lymphocytosis. These changes impair nutrient absorption and result in symptoms such as chronic diarrhea, weight loss, abdominal bloating, irritability, and iron-deficiency anemia. The small intestine—especially the proximal duodenum—is most affected, explaining the malabsorption of iron and other nutrients absorbed there. stem: A 26-year-old woman comes to the clinic for evaluation of chronic diarrhea, abdominal bloating, and unintentional weight loss. She reports that these symptoms have been present for the past year but have worsened over the last 4 months. The diarrhea occurs 4–6 times per day, often within a few hours after meals, and is described as loose, bulky, and foul-smelling. She notes increased bloating after eating pasta, bread, pastries, or certain breakfast cereals. Over the past few months, she has also experienced frequent fatigue and difficulty concentrating. She denies fevers, night sweats, or recent travel. The patient works as a preschool teacher and lives with her partner. She denies any recent sick contacts. She has not been hospitalized and has not used antibiotics in the past year. She has no pets and drinks municipal tap water. She does not smoke or use alcohol or illicit drugs. Her menstrual cycles are regular, but she has noticed heavier flow over the last several months. She has no history of chronic medical conditions or surgeries. She recalls having mouth sores intermittently over the past year, which she attributed to stress. She has also experienced mild, diffuse bone pain on occasion and notes that her fingernails have become more brittle. She has not changed her diet intentionally but has occasionally skipped meals due to bloating and discomfort. She has tried over-the-counter probiotics and fiber supplements without improvement. Her family history is notable for a maternal aunt with “intestinal issues” and a cousin diagnosed with type 1 diabetes. Her father has hypothyroidism. There is no family history of inflammatory bowel disease or colon cancer. On review of systems, she denies rash, but on further questioning, she recalls a prior episode of a mildly itchy rash on her elbows about a year ago that resolved without treatment. She has not had joint swelling, back pain, or visual changes. She does not have any urinary symptoms or lower extremity swelling. On examination, the patient is alert and cooperative but appears thin. Temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure 108/66 mm Hg, pulse 78/min, and respirations 14/min. Body mass index is 18.4 kg/m². Oral examination reveals a shallow, well-circumscribed ulcer on the left buccal mucosa. The abdomen is mildly distended with hyperactive bowel sounds and diffuse, mild tenderness to palpation, greatest in the upper abdomen. There is no hepatosplenomegaly or abdominal masses. Extremities show no edema, and neurologic examination is normal. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin of 10.8 g/d L, mean corpuscular volume of 74 f L, and ferritin of 8 ng/m L. Liver enzymes are normal. Thyroid function testing is normal. Stool cultures and ova/parasite studies are negative. Fecal leukocyte testing is positive. An upper endoscopy is performed and shows a scalloped appearance of the duodenal mucosa. A biopsy specimen is shown. Celiac disease results from an HLA-DQ2/DQ8–associated autoimmune response to deamidated gliadin, leading to T-cell–mediated mucosal injury and production of anti-t TG antibodies. Which of the following mechanisms most likely explains this patient’s disease?

options.A: Autoantibody formation against tissue transglutaminase after deamidation of dietary substrate
options.B: Bacterial superantigen–induced polyclonal T-cell activation in the intestinal mucosa
options.C: Ig A-mediated immune complex deposition in small-vessel walls causing mucosal ischemia
options.D: Mast cell degranulation with histamine release in response to allergen- bound Ig E
options.E: Th1 cell–mediated granulomatous inflammation of the terminal ileum with transmural injury

answer_text: Autoantibody formation against tissue transglutaminase after deamidation of dietary substrate
explanation: Celiac disease is an autoimmune-mediated enteropathy triggered by ingestion of gluten in genetically susceptible individuals, particularly those carrying the HLA-DQ2 or HLA-DQ8 haplotypes. Gluten, a protein found in wheat, barley, and rye, contains gliadin, which is resistant to complete digestion in the gastrointestinal tract. In the lamina propria of the small intestine, tissue transglutaminase (t TG) modifies gliadin through deamidation, converting certain glutamine residues into negatively charged glutamate residues. This deamidation increases gliadin’s affinity for binding to HLA-DQ2/DQ8 molecules on antigen-presenting cells, which present the gliadin peptides to CD4+ T helper cells. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.

stem: A 43-year-old woman presents with several months of intermittent abdominal pain and non-bloody diarrhea. She reports unintentional weight loss and increased stool frequency, particularly after meals. Laboratory studies show mild anemia and low serum vitamin D. Sudan Black B stain of the stool is positive for fat. Colonoscopy with terminal ileum biopsy reveals patchy areas of inflammation and granuloma formation. What is the most likely mechanism responsible for this patient’s diarrhea? Crohn disease affecting the terminal ileum can lead to bile acid malabsorption, resulting in secretory diarrhea and steatorrhea. Positive stool fat stain and granulomatous inflammation point toward impaired bile salt recycling. Bile acid sequestrants can help manage symptoms in affected patients.

options.C: Osmotic load from poorly absorbed carbohydrates

explanation: This patient presents with steatorrhea (positive Sudan Black B stain), weight loss, and histologic findings of non-caseating granulomas in the terminal ileum—features characteristic of Crohn disease, an inflammatory bowel disease that can involve any part of the GI tract from mouth to anus, but classically affects the terminal ileum. Terminal ileal involvement impairs bile acid reabsorption, which leads to bile salt loss into the colon. There, unabsorbed bile acids stimulate colonic fluid secretion and motility, causing secretory diarrhea and steatorrhea. This mechanism is called bile acid diarrhea, and is a major contributor to GI symptoms in patients with ileal Crohn disease. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: This patient presents with steatorrhea (positive Sudan Black B stain), weight loss, and histologic findings of non-caseating granulomas in the terminal ileum—features characteristic of Crohn disease, an inflammatory bowel disease that can involve any part of.

subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 31-year-old man comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressively worsening diarrhea and intermittent abdominal discomfort. He reports that his bowel movements are loose, occur 6–8 times daily, and are often accompanied by blood and mucus. The diarrhea sometimes wakes him at night. Over the past 3 months, he has lost 5 kg (11 lb) without trying. He has also noticed occasional low-grade fevers, fatigue, and decreased appetite. He denies recent travel, antibiotic use, or contact with anyone with similar symptoms. He has no history of camping, consumption of untreated water, or recent foreign travel. He does not smoke or drink alcohol and works as a software engineer. His only medication is a daily multivitamin. He has no known drug allergies. His father has a history of an “intestinal problem” that required surgery in his 40s, but the details are unclear. On review of systems, the patient reports occasional joint pain in his knees and ankles, especially in the morning, which improves throughout the day. He also describes redness and mild discomfort in his eyes over the past few weeks. He denies skin rashes, oral ulcers, or chronic back pain. On examination, the patient appears mildly pale but is in no acute distress. Temperature is 37.9°C (100.2°F), blood pressure 118/74 mm Hg, pulse 84/min, respirations 14/min. His abdomen is soft with mild tenderness in the lower quadrants, without rebound or guarding. There is no palpable hepatosplenomegaly or abdominal masses. Bowel sounds are present and normoactive. Rectal examination reveals gross blood mixed with stool. No perianal fistulas or abscesses are noted. Initial laboratory studies show mild normocytic anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Stool cultures and ova/parasite studies are negative. Ulcerative colitis involves autoimmune-mediated inflammation limited to the mucosa and submucosa of the colon, producing continuous lesions starting at the rectum and a “lead-pipe” appearance on barium studies. Which of the following mechanisms most likely underlies this patient’s condition?

options.A: Autoimmune-mediated epithelial damage limited to the colonic mucosa & submucosa
options.B: Cell-mediated transmural granulomatous inflammation of the gastrointestinal tract
options.C: Immune complex deposition in mesenteric vessels causing segmental ischemia
options.D: Mast cell–mediated histamine release with increased intestinal permeability
options.E: Th1 cytokine–driven delayed-type hypersensitivity reaction involving the terminal ileum

answer_text: Autoimmune-mediated epithelial damage limited to the colonic mucosa & submucosa
explanation: Ulcerative colitis (UC) is a chronic idiopathic inflammatory bowel disease in which the immune system mounts an abnormal response against colonic epithelial cells, likely triggered by the gut microbiota in genetically susceptible individuals. The inflammation begins in the rectum and extends proximally in a continuous fashion, never skipping regions, and is confined to the mucosa and submucosa. Unlike Crohn disease, which is transmural, UC’s inflammation is superficial, which influences both its complications and radiographic findings. The "lead-pipe" appearance on barium enema results from chronic mucosal injury, muscularis hypertrophy, and loss of haustra due to long-standing inflammation. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: Ulcerative colitis (UC) is a chronic idiopathic inflammatory bowel disease in which the immune system mounts an abnormal response against colonic epithelial cells, likely triggered by the gut microbiota in genetically susceptible individuals. The inflammation begins.

stem: A 64-year-old woman presents with progressive fatigue and difficulty concentrating over the past 2 months. Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy with biopsy reveals the histologic section shown, with the area labeled X indicating the affected cell type. Choice B: G cells are endocrine cells located mainly in the antrum of the stomach that secrete gastrin, a hormone that stimulates gastric acid secretion from parietal cells and promotes mucosal growth. In autoimmune gastritis, destruction of parietal cells leads to hypochlorhydria, which actually removes the negative feedback on gastrin production. This results in secondary hypergastrinemia, not decreased gastrin secretion. Therefore, a reduction in gastrin secretion would not be expected in this patient. Choice C: Chief cells are located deep in the gastric glands of the fundus and body and are responsible for secreting pepsinogen, the inactive precursor of the proteolytic enzyme pepsin. They have basophilic (purple) cytoplasm on H&E staining due to abundant rough endoplasmic reticulum used for protein synthesis. In autoimmune gastritis, the primary target is the parietal cell, not the chief cell. While pepsinogen secretion may decrease late in disease due to gland atrophy, early disease typically spares chief cell function. Choice D: D cells are located mainly in the gastric antrum and duodenum and secrete somatostatin, which inhibits gastric acid secretion, gastrin release, and pancreatic hormone secretion. In parietal cell–destroying conditions like autoimmune gastritis, somatostatin secretion is not primarily decreased. In fact, low gastric acidity can alter normal regulation, but the main defect is acid and intrinsic factor loss, not hormone suppression from D cells. Choice E: Surface mucous cells (aka foveolar cells) line the gastric mucosa and secrete mucus and bicarbonate to protect the stomach epithelium from autodigestion by gastric acid. These cells are most concentrated in the cardia and gastric antrum. While autoimmune gastritis primarily affects the gastric body/fundus and targets parietal cells in the oxyntic glands, surface mucous cells are generally preserved until advanced atrophy occurs. Therefore, a significant primary decrease in bicarbonate secretion is not the most likely finding here. Which of the following is most likely to be decreased in this patient?

options.E: Surface mucous neck (foveolar) cell bicarbonate secretion

explanation: The cell labeled X is a parietal cell of the gastric fundus/body mucosa. Parietal cells have large, round to pyramid-shaped bodies with eosinophilic (pink) cytoplasm due to their high mitochondrial content. They are responsible for: 1) Hydrochloric acid secretion via the H⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump on the apical membrane, maintaining gastric acidity. 2) Intrinsic factor secretion, which binds vitamin B₁₂ to allow its absorption in the terminal ileum. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: The cell labeled X is a parietal cell of the gastric fundus/body mucosa. Parietal cells have large, round to pyramid-shaped bodies with eosinophilic (pink) cytoplasm due to their high mitochondrial content.

subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a six-month history of an insidious, nonproductive cough and intermittent low-grade fevers. She reports increasing fatigue but denies night sweats, weight loss, or hemoptysis. She has no significant past medical history and is not on any medications. She works as a schoolteacher and has no known exposures to tuberculosis or other occupational hazards. On examination, her temperature is 37.8°C, and her oxygen saturation is 96% on room air. There are no crackles or wheezes on lung auscultation. Cardiac exam is normal, and there is no peripheral edema. She has several tender, erythematous nodules on her shins. A tuberculin skin test placed three days ago is negative. Chest radiography reveals symmetric enlargement of both hilar lymph nodes without focal infiltrates. A high-resolution CT confirms bihilar lymphadenopathy and mild interstitial thickening. Labs show normal renal function and no leukocytosis. An ophthalmologic exam is unremarkable. A transbronchial lymph node biopsy is scheduled to confirm the etiology of her granulomatous disease. Which of the following best represents the expected changes in this patient’s fecal calcium, 24,25-(OH)₂-vitamin D₃, and 1,25- (OH)₂-vitamin D₃ levels? Sarcoidosis causes increased extrarenal activation of vitamin D by macrophages in granulomas, leading to increased 1,25-D₃, decreased 24,25-D₃, and enhanced calcium absorption with low fecal calcium. Granuloma-induced hypervitaminosis D is a key metabolic signature of sarcoidosis.
explanation: Sarcoidosis is a granulomatous inflammatory disease characterized by non-caseating granulomas, most commonly affecting the lungs and lymph nodes. One of the important biochemical hallmarks of sarcoidosis is extrarenal production of 1α-hydroxylase by activated macrophages within the granulomas. This enzyme converts 25-hydroxyvitamin D (25-D₃) into the active form, 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D (1,25-D₃), independent of the kidney or PTH regulation. This increase in 1,25-D₃ leads to enhanced intestinal calcium absorption, resulting in decreased fecal calcium and potential hypercalcemia. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: Sarcoidosis is a granulomatous inflammatory disease characterized by non-caseating granulomas, most commonly affecting the lungs and lymph nodes. One of the important biochemical hallmarks of sarcoidosis is extrarenal production of 1α-hydroxylase by activated macrophages within the.

stem: A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic due to chronic epigastric discomfort and unintentional weight loss over the past several months. He describes the pain as dull and non-radiating, worsening after meals, and often accompanied by bloating and greasy, foul-smelling stools. He has no history of blood in stool, vomiting, or fevers. His past medical history is notable for multiple prior hospitalizations for acute pancreatitis, most recently eight months ago. He drinks six to eight beers daily and smokes one pack of cigarettes per day. He is not taking any regular medications and denies NSAID use. On exam, he appears thin but in no distress. BMI is 18.1 kg/m². There is mild epigastric tenderness without rebound or guarding. Laboratory testing reveals a normal serum calcium and lipase. Stool analysis is positive for fat and undigested muscle fibers. D-xylose test results are within normal limits. A CT scan of the abdomen is performed and shown below. Chronic pancreatitis causes pancreatic enzyme deficiency due to acinar atrophy and ductal calcification, especially in alcoholics. CT showing pancreatic calcifications + steatorrhea is highly diagnostic. D-xylose test is normal, helping rule out mucosal disorders like celiac or Crohn disease. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.B: Exocrine insufficiency due to acinar atrophy and intraductal calcification
options.C: Impaired bile salt resorption due to terminal ileal inflammation
options.D: Villous atrophy with intraepithelial lymphocytic infiltration
options.E: Zollinger-Ellison syndrome due to ectopic gastrin production

answer_text: Exocrine insufficiency due to acinar atrophy and intraductal calcification
explanation: This patient presents with chronic abdominal symptoms, steatorrhea, and a CT showing pancreatic calcifications, which is highly indicative of chronic pancreatitis. The most common cause in the US is chronic alcohol abuse, which this patient has, along with a history of recurrent acute pancreatitis episodes. Over time, chronic inflammation leads to fibrosis, loss of pancreatic acinar cells, and intraductal calcification. These structural changes lead to pancreatic enzyme deficiency, causing fat and protein malabsorption. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: This patient presents with chronic abdominal symptoms, steatorrhea, and a CT showing pancreatic calcifications, which is highly indicative of chronic pancreatitis. The most common cause in the US is chronic alcohol abuse, which this patient has.

subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 56-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his wife after he vomited a large amount of blood at home. He reports several weeks of progressive fatigue, early satiety, and intermittent mild abdominal discomfort. His past medical history includes long-standing alcohol use disorder, and he has been hospitalized in the past for ascites and hepatic encephalopathy. On examination, he appears pale and diaphoretic with a distended abdomen, shifting dullness, and multiple spider angiomas on his chest. Laboratory tests reveal anemia and thrombocytopenia. After fluid resuscitation, an upper endoscopy reveals dilated veins in the distal esophagus with signs of recent hemorrhage. This bleeding most likely results from dilation of submucosal vessels at a portosystemic anastomosis involving which of the following veins? The left gastric vein connects the esophageal veins to the portal. The USMLE really likes this as an answer for esophageal varices questions.
explanation: The esophageal veins drain in part into the left gastric vein, which in turn drains into the portal, which goes to the liver. In the setting of cirrhosis, where portal pressure is high, this backs up to the left gastric vein, and in turn, the esophageal veins, leading to varices, which are engorged and dilated esophageal veins. These fragile vessels can rupture and lead to life-threatening upper GI bleeding. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: The esophageal veins drain in part into the left gastric vein, which in turn drains into the portal, which goes to the liver. In the setting of cirrhosis, where portal pressure is high, this backs up.

stem: A 54-year-old man comes to the emergency department with sudden-onset severe abdominal pain that began 2 hours ago. He describes the pain as constant and diffuse, with no relieving or exacerbating factors. He has a history of long-standing dyspepsia for which he intermittently takes over-the-counter antacids. Over the past week, he has noted worsening epigastric burning after meals but did not seek medical attention. He does not smoke or drink alcohol. On examination, the patient is lying still and appears diaphoretic. Temperature is 38.2°C (100.8°F), blood pressure is 96/60 mm Hg, and pulse is 112/min. Abdominal examination reveals distention, generalized tenderness with guarding, and marked rebound tenderness; bowel sounds are absent. Upright chest radiograph shows free air under the diaphragm. Which of the following is the most likely site of the underlying pathology? Peptic ulcer disease most often involves the duodenum or stomach and is strongly linked to H pylori infection, chronic NSAID use, and smoking. Anterior duodenal ulcers are more likely to perforate, causing sudden-onset peritonitis and free subdiaphragmatic air, whereas posterior duodenal ulcers more often erode into the gastroduodenal artery and cause massive upper GI bleeding.
explanation: This patient’s presentation—sudden, severe abdominal pain with diffuse peritonitis, absent bowel sounds, hypotension, and free air under the diaphragm on upright chest radiograph—indicates perforation of a hollow viscus. In a patient with a history of dyspepsia and recent worsening epigastric burning, the most likely cause is a perforated peptic ulcer. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: This patient’s presentation—sudden, severe abdominal pain with diffuse peritonitis, absent bowel sounds, hypotension, and free air under the diaphragm on upright chest radiograph—indicates perforation of a hollow viscus. In a patient with a history of dyspepsia.

subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 47-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset severe upper abdominal pain that began 6 hours ago. The pain is constant, radiates to the back, and is associated with nausea and two episodes of non-bloody, non- bilious vomiting. She denies recent trauma, alcohol intake, or new medications. Her past medical history is significant for obesity and hyperlipidemia; she has no prior abdominal surgeries. She takes no daily medications. Family history is notable for gallstones in her mother. On examination, she is afebrile with a blood pressure of 128/78 mm Hg, pulse of 96/min, and respiratory rate of 20/min. She appears uncomfortable and is lying still on the stretcher. Abdominal examination reveals epigastric tenderness to deep palpation without guarding or rebound; bowel sounds are decreased. Murphy's sign is negative. Laboratory studies show: Amylase 1,120 U/L Lipase 2,600 U/L Alanine aminotransferase 172 U/L Aspartate aminotransferase 156 U/L Total bilirubin 2.1 mg/d L Abdominal ultrasound reveals multiple gallstones within the gallbladder and common bile duct dilation to 9 mm. Which of the following anatomic sites is most likely obstructed, leading to these findings? Choice B: The cystic duct connects the gallbladder neck to the common hepatic duct to form the common bile duct; obstruction here traps bile within the gallbladder and causes acute calculous cholecystitis with right upper quadrant pain, fever, and a positive Murphy sign. Because the obstruction is proximal to the common bile duct, the extrahepatic biliary tree does not dilate and pancreatic drainage is unaffected, so marked transaminase elevation and a dilated common bile duct with pancreatitis would not be expected. This patient’s negative Murphy sign, elevated ALT >150 U/L, and 9-mm CBD dilation indicate a more distal blockage than the cystic duct. Choice C: The hepatic veins drain blood from the liver into the inferior vena cava; obstruction produces Budd–Chiari syndrome with tender hepatomegaly, ascites, abdominal pain, and hepatic congestion rather than biliary dilatation. Enzymes typically reflect hepatocellular injury and congestion, not a cholestatic pattern, and there is no mechanism for simultaneous pancreatic outflow obstruction. Hepatic vein occlusion would not enlarge the common bile duct or provoke acute pancreatitis. Choice D: The pancreatic duct in the tail lies near the splenic hilum and carries pancreatic juice from the tail toward the head to join the main duct; obstruction limited to the tail would not impinge on the common bile duct, which courses in the hepatoduodenal ligament and merges with the main pancreatic duct near the head. A distal tail blockage could cause localized pancreatitis but would not produce common bile duct dilation or biliary enzyme elevation as seen here. The combination of pancreatitis plus CBD dilation localizes the obstruction to the shared outflow tract at the head/ampulla, not the tail. Choice E: The portal vein brings nutrient-rich blood from the splanchnic circulation to the liver; portal vein thrombosis causes portal hypertension with splenomegaly, varices, and possible ascites, while liver enzymes are often near normal and the biliary tree is not dilated. Portal venous obstruction does not block bile or pancreatic juice flow and therefore does not explain a 9-mm CBD with pancreatitis and cholestatic transaminase elevations.
explanation: The ampulla of Vater (also called the hepatopancreatic ampulla) is the anatomical site where the common bile duct and main pancreatic duct converge before draining into the second portion of the duodenum through the major duodenal papilla. In acute gallstone pancreatitis, a gallstone migrating from the gallbladder through the cystic duct and common bile duct can lodge at the ampulla of Vater, simultaneously obstructing both the biliary and pancreatic ducts. This obstruction leads to stasis of pancreatic secretions, premature activation of digestive enzymes within the pancreas, and autodigestion of pancreatic tissue, causing acute inflammation. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: The ampulla of Vater (also called the hepatopancreatic ampulla) is the anatomical site where the common bile duct and main pancreatic duct converge before draining into the second portion of the duodenum through the major duodenal.

subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 7-week-old girl is brought to the clinic by her parents due to ongoing jaundice and concerns about the color of her stools. They report that the jaundice was first noticed around the second week of life and has progressively worsened. Over the past two weeks, they’ve observed that her stools are light yellow to almost white, and her urine has become dark and stains the diaper. She feeds every 2–3 hours but has seemed fussier than usual and less vigorous during feeds. There is no vomiting, fever, or diarrhea. She was born full-term via spontaneous vaginal delivery without complications and has been exclusively breastfed. Newborn screening was reportedly normal, and there is no family history of liver disease. Growth parameters show her weight has dropped from the 50th to the 15th percentile over the past month. On physical examination, she is alert but irritable. Her sclerae are icteric, and her skin is deeply jaundiced. Abdominal exam reveals a firm liver edge palpable 5 cm below the right costal margin. No splenomegaly or ascites is noted. Laboratory evaluation shows a total bilirubin of 16 mg/d L with a direct component of 10.5 mg/d L. AST, ALT, and GGT are all elevated, and PT is mildly prolonged. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis? Biliary atresia should be suspected in infants with persistent jaundice beyond 2 weeks, direct hyperbilirubinemia, acholic stools, and dark urine. Early surgical intervention is crucial to prevent irreversible liver damage.
explanation: This infant has persistent jaundice beyond two weeks of life with elevated direct (conjugated) bilirubin, acholic stools, dark urine, hepatomegaly, and failure to thrive—all hallmark signs of biliary atresia. Biliary atresia is a progressive fibro-obliterative disorder of the extrahepatic bile ducts that presents in the first 2 months of life and is the most common indication for pediatric liver transplantation. The absence of bile flow leads to retention of conjugated bilirubin, causing dark urine and pale stools due to the lack of bilirubin excretion into the gut. Laboratory findings typically reveal a cholestatic picture, with elevated conjugated bilirubin and increased liver enzymes (especially GGT). The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: This infant has persistent jaundice beyond two weeks of life with elevated direct (conjugated) bilirubin, acholic stools, dark urine, hepatomegaly, and failure to thrive—all hallmark signs of biliary atresia. Biliary atresia is a progressive fibro-obliterative disorder.

subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 57-year-old woman presents to clinic with one week of yellowing of the skin and sclerae. She also notes her stools have become pale and her urine has turned unusually dark. She denies fever, abdominal pain, or nausea. Her appetite is normal, and she reports regular bowel movements. The patient underwent a laparoscopic cholecystectomy eight days ago for presumed symptomatic gallstones. She recovered well initially but began to notice progressive discoloration of her eyes and urine three days ago. She has no prior history of liver disease, alcohol use, or intravenous drug use. Her surgical records indicate that no intraoperative cholangiography was performed. Physical exam reveals scleral icterus and mild jaundice of the skin. There is no abdominal tenderness or hepatosplenomegaly. Surgical wounds are clean and healing well. Bowel sounds are present, and the abdomen is soft and nondistended. Rectal exam is deferred. The rest of her exam is unremarkable. Her vital signs are normal, and she remains afebrile. Laboratory results are pending. Which of the following best describes the expected findings in this patient?

options.A: ↓ Hematocrit, ↑ Indirect bilirubin, ↑ Direct bilirubin, ↑ Total bilirubin
options.B: ↔ Hematocrit, ↑ Indirect bilirubin, ↑ Direct bilirubin, ↑ Total bilirubin
options.C: ↔ Hematocrit, ↑ Indirect bilirubin, ↔ Direct bilirubin, ↑ Total bilirubin
options.D: ↔ Hematocrit, ↔ Indirect bilirubin, ↑ Direct bilirubin, ↑ Total bilirubin
options.E: ↔ Hematocrit, ↔ Indirect bilirubin, ↔ Direct bilirubin, ↔ Total bilirubin

answer_text: ↔ Hematocrit, ↔ Indirect bilirubin, ↑ Direct bilirubin, ↑ Total bilirubin
explanation: This patient presents one week after cholecystectomy with painless jaundice, pale stools, and dark urine—findings strongly suggestive of post-hepatic biliary obstruction, most commonly due to a retained common bile duct (CBD) stone, known as choledocholithiasis. While her gallbladder was removed, stones can remain in the biliary tree or migrate from the cystic duct postoperatively, particularly in the absence of intraoperative cholangiography, which is sometimes omitted in uncomplicated cholecystectomy cases. Retained stones can occlude the flow of bile, leading to a backup of conjugated (direct) bilirubin, which is water-soluble and spills into the bloodstream. This increase results in dark urine, as conjugated bilirubin is renally excreted, and pale or acholic stools, since no bilirubin-derived pigments (e.g., stercobilin) reach the intestine. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: This patient presents one week after cholecystectomy with painless jaundice, pale stools, and dark urine—findings strongly suggestive of post-hepatic biliary obstruction, most commonly due to a retained common bile duct (CBD) stone, known as choledocholithiasis. While.

stem: A 45-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of abdominal bloating, foul-smelling greasy stools, and unintentional weight loss. She has no significant past medical history but reports frequent episodes of fatigue and mild lower extremity swelling. On physical exam, she has pale conjunctivae and trace bilateral ankle edema. Lab results show hemoglobin of 10.2 g/d L and MCV 73 f L. A D-xylose test shows low urinary excretion of the sugar following oral administration. Which of the following best explains this patient’s findings? Celiac disease is an immune-mediated sensitivity to gluten that leads to villous atrophy and blunting in the small intestine, resulting in nutrient malabsorption. Common consequences include iron deficiency anemia (due to proximal small bowel involvement) and fat-soluble vitamin deficiencies.

options.A: Autoantibody-mediated enterocyte damage triggered by dietary antigen
options.B: Chronic inflammation of terminal ileum impairing bile acid reabsorption
options.C: Congenital mutation in apolipoprotein B leading to lipid transport failure
options.D: Disaccharidase deficiency at brush border causing malabsorption
options.E: Pancreatic enzyme deficiency causing maldigestion of macronutrients

answer_text: Autoantibody-mediated enterocyte damage triggered by dietary antigen
explanation: Celiac disease is an insensitivity to gluten (which contains gliadin), found in wheat, oats, rye, and barley. Consumption results in a mixed antibody and T-cell response resulting in flattening/blunting of the intestinal villi. This biopsy finding is exceedingly HY for USMLE. Patients will have antibodies against gliadin/gluten, which are aka anti-endomysial, as well as anti-tissue transglutaminase Ig A. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: Celiac disease is an insensitivity to gluten (which contains gliadin), found in wheat, oats, rye, and barley. Consumption results in a mixed antibody and T-cell response resulting in flattening/blunting of the intestinal villi.

subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 72-year-old man comes to the clinic with a 1-year history of progressive difficulty swallowing solids and liquids, frequent regurgitation of undigested food, and foul- smelling breath. His wife notes that he often coughs and sometimes wakes up at night choking. On exam, he has halitosis, gurgling in the neck with palpation, and no cervical lymphadenopathy. A barium swallow demonstrates a posterior outpouching in the hypopharyngeal region. This abnormality is most likely located between which of the following muscles? Zenker diverticulum is a false diverticulum of the hypopharynx due to impaired relaxation of the cricopharyngeus muscle, localizing between the cricopharyngeus and inferior pharyngeal constrictor. It presents with dysphagia, halitosis, regurgitation, and aspiration risk.
explanation: This patient has a Zenker diverticulum, a false diverticulum that occurs through Killian’s triangle, the weak area between the thyropharyngeus portion of the inferior pharyngeal constrictor and the cricopharyngeus muscle. Dysfunction of the cricopharyngeus, which fails to relax properly during swallowing, increases intraluminal pressure and causes herniation of mucosa and submucosa. Clinically, patients present with dysphagia, regurgitation of undigested food, halitosis (due to trapped food debris), chronic cough, and risk of aspiration pneumonia. The barium swallow is the diagnostic study of choice and reveals a posteriorly directed outpouching just above the esophagus. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: This patient has a Zenker diverticulum, a false diverticulum that occurs through Killian’s triangle, the weak area between the thyropharyngeus portion of the inferior pharyngeal constrictor and the cricopharyngeus muscle. Dysfunction of the cricopharyngeus, which fails.

subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 27-year-old woman comes to the emergency department due to vomiting and hematemesis. She reports that over the past 24 hours, she has had repeated episodes of non-bloody vomiting. Earlier today, she vomited once more and noticed a small amount of bright red blood mixed with gastric contents. She denies abdominal pain, chest pain, or shortness of breath. She has a history of bulimia nervosa and has had multiple similar episodes in the past but never with blood. She does not drink alcohol. On examination, her vitals are within normal limits, and she appears well- perfused and comfortable. The abdomen is soft, non-distended, and non-tender. An upright chest x-ray is normal. Which of the following best explains this patient's clinical presentation?

options.A: Disruption of submucosal veins in the distal esophagus due to portal hypertension
options.B: Full-thickness rupture of the esophageal wall due to elevated intraesophageal pressure
options.C: Incomplete mucosal tear at the gastroesophageal junction
options.D: Ischemic necrosis of the gastric fundus due to compromised arterial supply
options.E: Ulceration of the gastric mucosa from chronic acid exposure and reduced mucosal defenses

answer_text: Incomplete mucosal tear at the gastroesophageal junction
explanation: This patient presents with mild hematemesis following episodes of retching, in the setting of an eating disorder (bulimia nervosa) — a classic setup for a Mallory-Weiss tear. This condition occurs when repetitive forceful vomiting or retching causes a longitudinal mucosal laceration at the gastroesophageal junction, specifically on the gastric side or distal esophagus. The tear is limited to the mucosa or submucosa, and the bleeding is typically mild and self-limited, often presenting as streaks or small amounts of bright red blood in the vomitus. Mallory-Weiss tears are most commonly associated with alcoholics, but USMLE also involve non-alcoholic patients with retching, such as those with bulimia, hyperemesis gravidarum, or GI illness. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: This patient presents with mild hematemesis following episodes of retching, in the setting of an eating disorder (bulimia nervosa) — a classic setup for a Mallory-Weiss tear. This condition occurs when repetitive forceful vomiting or retching.

subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 64-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of intermittent chest discomfort and a sensation of fullness after eating small meals. She also reports occasional shortness of breath after large dinners and says she sometimes feels “pressure” in the middle of her chest when lying down. The discomfort is not related to exertion and is not relieved by antacids. She denies dysphagia to solids or liquids but notes that she occasionally feels bloated after eating and has mild early satiety. She has no history of gastroesophageal reflux disease, vomiting, hematemesis, or melena. She takes lisinopril for hypertension and no other medications. She does not smoke, drink alcohol, or use illicit drugs. The patient’s BMI is 27 kg/m². Her temperature is 37.1°C (98.8°F), blood pressure is 138/80 mm Hg, and pulse is 76/min. Cardiopulmonary examination is unremarkable. Abdominal examination reveals no tenderness, organomegaly, or masses. Bowel sounds are normal. A chest x-ray obtained for unrelated reasons last month showed an air-fluid level in the posterior mediastinum. Upper gastrointestinal barium swallow performed today demonstrates a portion of the gastric fundus located above the diaphragm, with the gastroesophageal junction in its normal subdiaphragmatic position. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis? Paraesophageal hiatal hernia is characterized by herniation of the gastric fundus alongside the esophagus with the GEJ in its normal location. It presents with mechanical symptoms rather than reflux and carries a high risk of complications, making recognition crucial. Diagnosis is made with barium swallow or endoscopy, and surgical repair is indicated for symptomatic or large hernias.
explanation: A paraesophageal hiatal hernia occurs when part of the stomach, usually the gastric fundus, herniates through the esophageal hiatus into the thoracic cavity alongside the esophagus, while the gastroesophageal junction (GEJ) remains in its normal anatomic location. The primary mechanism involves laxity of the phrenoesophageal membrane and widening of the diaphragmatic hiatus that preferentially allows the stomach to herniate without displacing the GEJ. This type is less common than the sliding hiatal hernia but is clinically important because the herniated stomach can become incarcerated or strangulated, leading to ischemia, necrosis, or even gastric perforation. Unlike sliding hernias, which often present with reflux symptoms, paraesophageal hernias more commonly cause mechanical symptoms such as early satiety, postprandial fullness, chest discomfort, and, in some cases, respiratory compromise from gastric compression of the lungs. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: A paraesophageal hiatal hernia occurs when part of the stomach, usually the gastric fundus, herniates through the esophageal hiatus into the thoracic cavity alongside the esophagus, while the gastroesophageal junction (GEJ) remains in its normal anatomic.

subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 59-year-old man comes to the gastroenterology clinic for evaluation of worsening gastroesophageal reflux symptoms. He reports a 2-year history of retrosternal burning after meals and occasional regurgitation of sour-tasting fluid into his mouth, which is particularly noticeable when lying down at night. Initially, symptoms occurred once every few weeks but have become almost nightly over the past 6 months. He occasionally experiences a sensation of food “coming back up” without vomiting and says that sleeping propped up on several pillows provides partial relief. He denies dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, hematemesis, or melena. The patient works as an accountant and spends most of his day at a desk. He is obese, with a sedentary lifestyle. He enjoys snacking at night, particularly on ginger crackers and diet lemon-lime soda while reading historical nonfiction. He notes that these snacks often worsen his burning sensation, which prompted him to seek medical attention. He has tried over-the-counter antacids with temporary relief but no lasting improvement. He also has a chronic cough from asthma, for which he uses an albuterol inhaler as needed. He does not smoke, drinks alcohol socially, and has no known food allergies. His father developed “bad heartburn” in his 60s and was told he had a hernia near his stomach. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals an overweight man in no acute distress. Cardiac and lung examinations are normal. Abdominal examination shows mild epigastric tenderness without rebound or guarding. There is no hepatosplenomegaly or palpable mass. Rectal examination is normal, and stool is guaiac-negative. Upper endoscopy demonstrates mild erosive esophagitis without evidence of Barrett esophagus. Barium swallow shows a sliding hiatal hernia. Which of the following best describes the primary mechanism responsible for this patient’s condition? Sliding hiatal hernia results from circumferential stretching and loss of tension in the phrenoesophageal membrane, leading to upward displacement of the gastroesophageal junction and cardia into the thorax. This weakens LES function and promotes gastroesophageal reflux, particularly in settings of obesity, chronic coughing, and late-night dietary triggers.

options.A: Circumferential laxity and elongation of the phrenoesophageal membrane with loss of gastroesophageal junction anchoring
options.B: Complete fibrotic obliteration of the esophageal myenteric plexus due to autoimmune ganglionitis
options.C: Congenital absence of the diaphragmatic crura at the esophageal hiatus
options.D: Hyperplastic thickening of the lower esophageal muscularis propria due to chronic acid exposure
options.E: Incomplete fusion of the right and left diaphragmatic domes during embryogenesis

answer_text: Circumferential laxity and elongation of the phrenoesophageal membrane with loss of gastroesophageal junction anchoring
explanation: Sliding hiatal hernia is the most common type of hiatal hernia, accounting for over 90% of cases. It occurs when both the gastroesophageal junction (GEJ) and the proximal stomach migrate above the diaphragm through the esophageal hiatus. The key anatomical support that maintains the GEJ in its subdiaphragmatic position is the phrenoesophageal membrane, a thin yet strong connective tissue sheet that attaches the distal esophagus to the diaphragm. In sliding hiatal hernia, this membrane undergoes circumferential stretching, thinning, and loss of elasticity, typically due to repetitive stress from increased intra-abdominal pressure (eg, obesity, chronic cough, straining, pregnancy) and age- related connective tissue changes. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: Sliding hiatal hernia is the most common type of hiatal hernia, accounting for over 90% of cases. It occurs when both the gastroesophageal junction (GEJ) and the proximal stomach migrate above the diaphragm through the esophageal.

subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 66-year-old man presents for evaluation following unintentional weight loss and intermittent abdominal discomfort over the past 3 months. He reports changes in bowel habits, including occasional loose stools and a sensation of incomplete evacuation. He denies overt rectal bleeding but describes dark stools on a few occasions. Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes and hypertension, both well controlled. He worked as a carpenter for 40 years and quit smoking 10 years ago after a 30-pack-year history. Colonoscopy reveals an ulcerated, obstructing mass in the ascending colon, and biopsy confirms moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. CT scan shows multiple hypodense lesions in the right hepatic lobe and several small pulmonary nodules in both lungs. Laboratory testing reveals iron deficiency anemia. The patient is referred to oncology for further management. Which of the following structures most likely transmitted tumor cells from the primary site to the lungs? G. Superior vena cava Choices A and G: The azygos vein and superior vena cava (SVC) are part of the systemic venous system draining the thoracic wall, upper extremities, and upper body. The azygos vein serves as a collateral pathway and empties into the SVC, which returns blood to the right atrium from the head, neck, and upper limbs. However, neither structure is connected to the venous drainage of the gastrointestinal tract or liver. Since metastasis from right-sided colon cancer typically proceeds through the portal system to the liver and then into systemic circulation via the hepatic veins and inferior vena cava, the azygos vein and SVC are not involved in this pathway and do not account for pulmonary spread in this patient. Choices B, E and F: The bronchial arteries are small systemic arteries that provide oxygenated blood to the bronchial tree and supporting tissues of the lungs. The pulmonary artery delivers deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle to the lungs, while the pulmonary veins return oxygenated blood to the left atrium. These vessels are confined to the pulmonary circulation and are not involved in the hematogenous spread of malignancies originating in distant organs like the colon. Because metastatic tumor cells travel through systemic venous routes, these pulmonary vessels are not part of the dissemination pathway in this case. Choice C: This vessel primarily drains blood from the left side of the colon, including the descending colon, sigmoid colon, and upper rectum. It joins the splenic vein, which then merges with the superior mesenteric vein to form the portal vein. While the inferior mesenteric vein plays an important role in venous drainage of the distal colon, it is not involved in the drainage of the right colon, which is where this patient’s primary tumor originated. Therefore, this route does not account for the spread of cancer to the liver or lungs in this case.
explanation: The liver receives dual blood supply from the portal vein and hepatic artery. When colorectal cancer metastasizes, it typically spreads to the liver first via the portal venous system. Once liver metastases develop, tumor cells may invade hepatic veins, which directly drain into the inferior vena cava (IVC). The IVC then delivers venous blood to the right atrium of the heart, and from there, tumor emboli can travel through the pulmonary arteries into the lungs, leading to pulmonary metastases. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: The liver receives dual blood supply from the portal vein and hepatic artery. When colorectal cancer metastasizes, it typically spreads to the liver first via the portal venous system.

subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 48-year-old woman comes to the clinic for evaluation of mild right-sided abdominal discomfort that has been ongoing for several weeks. She denies nausea, vomiting, weight loss, or changes in bowel habits. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. Her BMI is 35 kg/m². She has a history of type 2 diabetes and hyperlipidemia, both of which are suboptimally controlled. She reports being mostly sedentary and admits to irregular dietary habits. She takes metformin and a statin but has not seen her endocrinologist in over a year. Vitals: Temperature 98.4°F (36.9°C), Blood Pressure 142/90 mm Hg, Pulse 82/min, Respiratory Rate 16/min. Physical examination shows mild hepatomegaly without tenderness. No stigmata of chronic liver disease are noted. Lab results show: AST 52 U/L ALT 63 U/L ALP 90 U/L Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/d L Hb A1c 7.9% LDL 158 mg/d L Triglycerides 260 mg/d L Hepatitis B surface antigen Negative Hepatitis C antibody Negative Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis? Nonalcoholic steatohepatitis should be suspected in patients with obesity, insulin resistance, and mildly elevated transaminases. The ALT > AST pattern in the absence of alcohol or viral hepatitis supports the diagnosis. Many patients with metabolic syndrome develop hepatic steatosis even with initially normal labs.
explanation: This patient presents with metabolic risk factors including obesity, type 2 diabetes, and dyslipidemia, along with asymptomatic hepatomegaly and mild elevation of transaminases—particularly with an ALT > AST pattern—which is characteristic of metabolic dysfunction-associated liver disease. Nonalcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH), now classified under metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD), is a common progression of hepatic steatosis seen in patients with features of metabolic syndrome. It is often discovered incidentally due to elevated liver enzymes in the absence of alcohol use or viral hepatitis. While many patients begin with normal labs, mild transaminase elevation is a common early laboratory sign once inflammation develops. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: This patient presents with metabolic risk factors including obesity, type 2 diabetes, and dyslipidemia, along with asymptomatic hepatomegaly and mild elevation of transaminases—particularly with an ALT > AST pattern—which is characteristic of metabolic dysfunction-associated liver disease..

subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 34-year-old man comes to the clinic due to persistent fatigue and intermittent right upper quadrant discomfort. He reports experiencing several months of loose, bloody stools with occasional abdominal cramping. He denies recent travel, sick contacts, or use of over-the-counter medications. Physical examination shows mild hepatomegaly without tenderness. Laboratory studies reveal total bilirubin of 2.5 mg/d L, alkaline phosphatase of 610 IU/L, AST of 65 IU/L, and ALT of 70 IU/L. Colonoscopy performed last month showed continuous inflammation extending from the rectum to the transverse colon, and biopsy confirmed crypt abscesses and mucosal ulceration. Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) reveals multifocal strictures and dilations of the intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts. He tests negative for antimitochondrial antibodies. He does not drink alcohol. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis? Primary sclerosing cholangitis is a HY extra-intestinal manifestation of ulcerative colitis, presenting with high direct bilirubin and ALP. The bile ducts can be beaded in appearance and p-ANCA can be positive.
explanation: Ulcerative colitis (UC) is a chronic inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) characterized by continuous mucosal/submucosal inflammation of the colon, beginning in the rectum and extending proximally in a contiguous pattern. It typically presents in young adults with bloody diarrhea, abdominal cramping, urgency, and tenesmus (the intractable feeling of needing to defecate), though some patients may have more subtle or intermittent symptoms. Colonoscopy for UC shows pseudopolyps and crypt abscesses. Biopsy shows inflammation limited to the mucosa and submucosa. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
