subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 43-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of vague abdominal discomfort and recent unintentional weight loss. She notes easy bruising over the past few weeks. She also reports occasional nausea and fatigue. Physical exam reveals mild jaundice and scattered ecchymoses. Laboratory studies show: Prothrombin time 20 seconds Partial thromboplastin time 35 seconds Damage to which of the following cell types is most likely responsible for this patient's findings? PT is the most sensitive marker of impaired liver function and is superior to ALT and AST as a prognostic indicator in liver failure. In contrast, ALT and AST more directly reflect hepatocellular damage, not function. PT is the answer on USMLE if they ask which most directly reflects liver failure.
explanation: Hepatocytes synthesize most of the clotting factors (especially II, V, VII, IX, X) and anti-clotting proteins C+S. Factors VIII and v WD are made by endothelial cells outside the liver. Factor VII has the shortest half-life (~4-6 hours). It is part of the extrinsic pathway that we monitor with PT. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: Hepatocytes synthesize most of the clotting factors (especially II, V, VII, IX, X) and anti-clotting proteins C+S. Factors VIII and v WD are made by endothelial cells outside the liver.

subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 64-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden right groin pain. She reports a 3-month history of a bulge in the same region that would often appear with standing or coughing and spontaneously reduce. Today, however, the bulge became painful and could not be reduced. She denies nausea or vomiting. Her medical history includes three vaginal deliveries and chronic constipation. On examination, she has a tender, non- reducible mass located inferior to the inguinal ligament and lateral to the pubic tubercle. The mass does not extend into the labia. Bowel sounds are absent over the mass. Vital signs are stable. Which of the following best describes the pathophysiology of her condition? Femoral hernias occur below the inguinal ligament through the femoral canal and are more common in women. Their narrow neck makes them more likely to incarcerate or strangulate than inguinal hernias.

options.A: Failure of processus vaginalis closure, allowing herniationthrough inguinal canal
options.B: Fascial defect in the Spigelian aponeurosis lateral to rectus abdominis
options.C: Incomplete closure of the umbilical ring with increasedintra-abdominal pressure
options.D: Protrusion through the femoral canal inferior to inguinal ligament
options.E: Weakness of transversalis fascia within Hesselbach triangle

answer_text: Protrusion through the femoral canal inferior to inguinal ligament
explanation: A femoral hernia occurs when intraabdominal contents herniate through the femoral canal, which lies inferior to the inguinal ligament and medial to the femoral vein. This canal is narrow and rigid, predisposing to strangulation and incarceration, which are surgical emergencies. Femoral hernias occur more often in women due to a wider pelvis and weaker pelvic musculature, especially in the setting of increased intraabdominal pressure such as pregnancy, obesity, or chronic constipation. These hernias are anatomically distinguished by their position below the inguinal ligament and lateral to the pubic tubercle. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: A femoral hernia occurs when intraabdominal contents herniate through the femoral canal, which lies inferior to the inguinal ligament and medial to the femoral vein. This canal is narrow and rigid, predisposing to strangulation and incarceration.

subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 6-month-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his parents because of a "lopsided" scrotum that grows more asymmetric when he cries. He was born at term via an uncomplicated vaginal delivery and has been feeding and growing normally. On examination, a soft, reducible mass is noted in the right hemiscrotum that enlarges with increased intra-abdominal pressure. The mass transilluminates poorly. Which of the following best describes the anatomic relations of this patient's condition? J . I. Medial to superior epigastric artery; superior to inguinal ligament H. Medial to superior epigastric artery; inferior to inguinal ligament G . Both inguinal hernias are superior to the inguinal ligament. Indirect inguinal hernia is lateral to the inferior epigastric artery; direct inguinal hernia is medial to it. Femoral hernias are more common in women and have higher risk of strangulation.

options.A: Lateral to inferior epigastric artery; inferior to inguinal ligament
options.B: Lateral to inferior epigastric artery; superior to inguinal ligament
options.C: Medial to inferior epigastric artery; inferior to inguinal ligament
options.D: Medial to inferior epigastric artery; superior to inguinal ligament
options.E: Medial to superior epigastric artery; superior to inguinal ligament

answer_text: Lateral to inferior epigastric artery; superior to inguinal ligament
explanation: The child has an indirect inguinal hernia, which is a herniation of the small bowel lateral to the inferior epigastric artery and superior to the inguinal ligament. It passes through the deep inguinal ring and often through the superficial inguinal ring into the scrotum. The mechanism USMLE likes is patent processus vaginalis. This is the same mechanism that causes hydrocele, although most infants with patent processus vaginalis do not go on to develop either hydrocele or indirect inguinal hernia. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: The child has an indirect inguinal hernia, which is a herniation of the small bowel lateral to the inferior epigastric artery and superior to the inguinal ligament. It passes through the deep inguinal ring and often.

subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 41-year-old man comes to the clinic with a 3-day history of severe rectal pain. The pain is constant, worsens when sitting, and is associated with a tender swelling he noticed near the anal opening. He denies abdominal pain, diarrhea, or weight loss. There is no history of rectal bleeding except for a small streak of bright red blood on toilet paper yesterday. He has chronic constipation and often strains during bowel movements. He has no significant medical conditions, does not take medications, and does not smoke or drink alcohol. On examination, a tense, bluish nodule is seen at the anal margin, and palpation causes marked tenderness. Rectal examination is otherwise unremarkable, and there are no internal masses. Which of the following arteries is the primary source of blood supply to the site of this patient’s lesion? External hemorrhoids occur below the dentate line, are painful due to somatic innervation, and receive blood supply from branches of the internal pudendal artery (internal iliac). Internal hemorrhoids occur above the dentate line, are usually painless, and are supplied by the superior rectal artery (inferior mesenteric).
explanation: This patient has a painful, bluish, tender swelling at the anal verge consistent with an external hemorrhoid. Hemorrhoids are vascular cushions that become pathologic when engorged, thrombosed, or symptomatic. The anorectal canal has a clear anatomic division at the dentate (pectinate) line, which determines the type of hemorrhoid, blood supply, venous drainage, lymphatics, and innervation. External hemorrhoids arise below the dentate line, where the overlying tissue is stratified squamous epithelium innervated by somatic sensory fibers (inferior rectal nerves, from the pudendal nerve). The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: This patient has a painful, bluish, tender swelling at the anal verge consistent with an external hemorrhoid. Hemorrhoids are vascular cushions that become pathologic when engorged, thrombosed, or symptomatic.

subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 29-year-old man presents with worsening crampy abdominal pain and progressive bloating that began insidiously several days ago and has become more constant today. He reports repeated nonbloody, nonbilious vomiting and has not passed flatus or stool since yesterday afternoon. Over the past few years he has had intermittent loose stools and vague right-lower-quadrant discomfort that waxes and wanes with periods of fatigue and decreased appetite. He denies prior abdominal operations, opioid use, recent travel, or ingestion of unusual foods. His weight has fluctuated by several kilograms during recent months, and he occasionally notes small perianal skin breaks that sting but heal without drainage. On examination he appears volume depleted with a distended, tympanic abdomen that is tender diffusely without rebound or guarding. Bowel sounds are high-pitched and come in bursts separated by quiet intervals. Rectal examination shows an empty vault without masses. Basic laboratory studies reveal mild leukocytosis and iron-deficiency anemia; serum chemistries are otherwise unremarkable. Abdominal imaging reveals multiple dilated loops of small bowel with air–fluid levels and a discrete transition point in the right lower quadrant. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient’s symptoms? Rigler triad. Choice E: Paralytic ileus is a functional motility arrest caused by postoperative neural inhibition, opioids, electrolyte disturbances (especially hypokalemia or hypomagnesemia), or systemic inflammation. Pain is typically mild and diffuse, bowel sounds are hypoactive or absent, and radiography shows uniform gaseous dilatation of both small and large bowel with air to the rectum and no transition point. Treatment is supportive—NPO, IV fluids, electrolyte correction, and opioid minimization—with spontaneous resolution over days as motility returns. Unlike mechanical obstruction, ileus carries no risk of strangulation from a fixed choke point. In this case, high-pitched intermittent sounds, obstipation, and a discrete RLQ transition point in a patient with a chronic Crohn history indicate a fixed fibrostenotic stricture rather than global hypoperistalsis.
explanation: A young adult with years of intermittent loose stools, weight fluctuation, right-lower-quadrant discomfort, iron-deficiency anemia, and occasional perianal symptoms now presents with high-pitched obstructive bowel sounds, obstipation, and imaging that localizes a transition point in the right lower quadrant. This pattern fits small-bowel obstruction in the setting of long-standing ileal Crohn disease, in which progressive fixed narrowing of the lumen creates a mechanical blockage. The absence of prior operations and the chronic inflammatory history orient the diagnosis toward an intrinsic small-bowel lesion produced by the disease itself. Chronic transmural inflammation in Crohn disease activates macrophages, Th1/Th17 pathways, and profibrotic cytokines (eg, TNF-α, IL-12/23, TGF-β), driving myofibroblast proliferation and excessive extracellular matrix deposition within the submucosa and muscularis. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: A young adult with years of intermittent loose stools, weight fluctuation, right-lower-quadrant discomfort, iron-deficiency anemia, and occasional perianal symptoms now presents with high-pitched obstructive bowel sounds, obstipation, and imaging that localizes a transition point in the.

subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 27-year-old man presents for evaluation prior to a planned drainage procedure for a perianal fluid collection that has developed gradually over the past week. He reports experiencing loose stools for several months, occasionally with abdominal discomfort that fluctuates in intensity. He denies recent travel, antibiotic use, or dietary changes. His weight has been slowly decreasing over the last year, which he attributes to poor appetite and irregular meals. Review of systems is notable for fatigue, intermittent low-grade fevers, and occasional soreness in the mouth that resolves on its own. Physical examination shows a thin male with mild tenderness in the lower abdomen and a small, tender area of fluctuance near the anal verge. Digital rectal examination elicits discomfort. Initial laboratory tests show mild anemia and a slightly elevated inflammatory marker. Imaging reveals a perirectal fluid collection suggestive of an abscess. Further evaluation is planned to clarify the underlying diagnosis and determine the most appropriate site for biopsy. What is the best site to obtain a diagnostic biopsy in this patient? Crohn disease most commonly affects the terminal ileum, making it the preferred site for diagnostic biopsy. Histologic features such as transmural inflammation and noncaseating granulomas are most likely to be found there. Rectal sparing helps distinguish Crohn disease from ulcerative colitis.
explanation: This patient presents with symptoms and signs suggestive of Crohn disease (CD), a chronic, idiopathic inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) characterized by transmural inflammation that can affect any part of the gastrointestinal (GI) tract, most commonly involving the terminal ileum. Key clinical clues include chronic, fluctuating abdominal discomfort, weight loss, non-bloody diarrhea, perianal disease (e.g., abscess or fistula), low-grade fever, and extraintestinal manifestations like aphthous ulcers (mouth sores). The presence of a perirectal abscess and long-standing constitutional symptoms supports a chronic, penetrating inflammatory process typical of Crohn disease. Biopsy of the ileum is the best approach for confirming the diagnosis in this clinical context. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: This patient presents with symptoms and signs suggestive of Crohn disease (CD), a chronic, idiopathic inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) characterized by transmural inflammation that can affect any part of the gastrointestinal (GI) tract, most commonly involving.

subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 74-year-old man comes to the emergency department because of recurrent episodes of painless bright red blood per rectum over the past month. Each episode lasts 1–2 days and resolves spontaneously. He denies abdominal pain, diarrhea, constipation, or weight loss. His medical history includes chronic kidney disease and severe aortic stenosis. He takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. On examination, his temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure is 128/70 mm Hg, pulse is 80/min, and respirations are 14/min. Abdominal examination is benign. Digital rectal examination shows gross blood, but no masses are palpable. Laboratory testing reveals a hemoglobin of 9.8 g/d L and mean corpuscular volume of 82 f L. The patient undergoes colonoscopy during an asymptomatic period, which does not reveal any masses, ulcers, or active bleeding sites. Given the persistence of intermittent bleeding and normal colonoscopic findings, a nuclear medicine-tagged red blood cell scan is performed and suggests bleeding from the cecum. A repeat colonoscopy during active bleeding demonstrates small, flat, cherry-red lesions with ectatic vessels in the cecum. Which of the following best explains this patient’s condition? Angiodysplasia is an acquired vascular malformation of the cecum and ascending colon that causes painless, intermittent lower GI bleeding in older adults. Lesions may not be visible on colonoscopy unless bleeding is active, making repeat or targeted evaluation essential.

options.A: Arteriovenous malformations of the mucosa and submucosa due to degenerative vascular changes
options.B: Autoimmune-mediated crypt destruction and chronic mucosal ulceration of the colon
options.C: Capillary telangiectasias from mutation in the ENG gene affecting TGF- beta signaling
options.D: Inflammatory destruction of terminal arterioles with fibrinoid necrosis
options.E: Venous hypertension in rectal submucosa from prolonged increased intra-abdominal pressure

answer_text: Arteriovenous malformations of the mucosa and submucosa due to degenerative vascular changes
explanation: Angiodysplasia is an acquired vascular malformation of the gastrointestinal tract characterized by dilated, tortuous submucosal veins, venules, and capillaries. These lesions most often occur in the cecum and ascending colon, areas subjected to high wall tension. The proposed mechanism involves intermittent obstruction of submucosal veins during peristaltic contractions, leading to dilation and eventual formation of abnormal arteriovenous communications. Over years, these thin-walled vessels become fragile and prone to rupture, causing episodic bleeding. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: Angiodysplasia is an acquired vascular malformation of the gastrointestinal tract characterized by dilated, tortuous submucosal veins, venules, and capillaries. These lesions most often occur in the cecum and ascending colon, areas subjected to high wall tension.

subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 68-year-old woman dies in the hospital after suffering a massive myocardial infarction. An autopsy is performed to evaluate the extent of her coronary artery disease and to document other comorbidities. Her heart is found to have severe atherosclerotic narrowing of all three major coronary arteries. The left anterior descending artery has a 95% occlusion with evidence of thrombus formation. The posterior descending artery has moderate narrowing. The myocardium shows a large area of transmural infarction. In addition to the cardiac findings, several other organs are examined. The lungs show mild emphysematous changes, likely related to her history of tobacco use. Her liver is mildly steatotic, which her physicians had attributed to long-standing obesity. The kidneys show arterionephrosclerosis. The uterus and ovaries are atrophic, consistent with her postmenopausal age. When the pathologist examines the large intestine, an additional abnormality is noted. The sigmoid colon appears irregular and somewhat thickened. Careful inspection reveals multiple small, round outpouchings ranging from 0.5 to 1 cm. These outpouchings are located along the antimesenteric border between the teniae coli. They are filled with inspissated stool and are surrounded by hypertrophied smooth muscle bands. On closer examination, it is clear that these lesions represent herniations of mucosa and submucosa through the muscular layer. Microscopy of one of these lesions shows attenuated colonic mucosa, with no evidence of epithelial dysplasia or malignant transformation. The muscularis propria is intact but interrupted at the site of the pouch, where the mucosa protrudes. The patient’s past medical history is significant for long-standing constipation, for which she frequently used laxatives. She also had a 25-year history of hypertension. Her family reports that she often complained of left lower quadrant abdominal discomfort. She had been hospitalized once for an episode of diverticulitis five years prior. She was treated with antibiotics at that time, with full recovery. Her social history is notable for frequent international travel. As a young adult, she spent two years working in rural India as a volunteer teacher. Later in life, she worked as a travel journalist, visiting multiple countries across Africa, South America, and Europe. She wrote extensively about public health conditions in different parts of the world. Her occupations included teaching, writing, and later serving as a tour guide. Despite these varied experiences, she had no history of parasitic infection or tropical illness. Her dietary history included frequent consumption of refined, low-fiber foods while traveling. She often joked with her family that her “Western diet” was easier to maintain abroad than local diets. Her physicians had repeatedly encouraged her to increase her dietary fiber, but she often ignored this advice. She worked at a desk for long stretches when writing, which contributed to her sedentary lifestyle. Constipation worsened with age. At autopsy, these sigmoid lesions are carefully documented. The pathologist notes that they are not true diverticula because they do not involve all layers of the bowel wall. Instead, they consist only of mucosa and submucosa herniating through areas of weakness in the muscularis propria. No evidence of carcinoma or polypoid lesions is found in the colon. Which of the following mechanisms best explains the formation of these lesions in the colon? Colonic diverticulosis represents false diverticula, where mucosa and submucosa herniate through the muscularis propria due to chronically elevated intraluminal pressure, especially in the sigmoid colon.

options.A: Abnormal apoptosis of colonic smooth muscle

explanation: Colonic diverticulosis is characterized by the formation of false diverticula, which involve only the mucosa and submucosa herniating through weak points in the muscularis propria. These weak points correspond to sites where vasa recta penetrate the circular muscle layer. The driving force for their formation is chronically elevated intraluminal pressure, most often in the sigmoid colon, which has the narrowest diameter and thus generates the highest pressures during peristalsis. Risk factors include a low-fiber diet, constipation, obesity, and advanced age, all of which contribute to years of straining and increased wall stress. stem: A 54-year-old man presents with worsening diffuse abdominal discomfort and persistent vomiting over the last 18 hours. He reports progressive bloating, minimal oral intake, and no passage of gas or stool since last night. His history includes an open appendectomy 8 years ago; he takes no daily medications and drinks fluids inconsistently. He denies fever, hematemesis, melena, chest pain, or urinary symptoms. On arrival he appears uncomfortable and mildly dehydrated. Temperature is 37.2 °C (99.0 °F), blood pressure 102/66 mm Hg, pulse 112/min, and respirations 20/min. The abdomen is distended and tympanic with diffuse mild tenderness; high- pitched rushes are heard intermittently followed by quiet intervals. No groin or incisional bulges are palpable, and rectal exam shows an empty vault. Intravenous fluids are begun, and a plain upright abdominal film is obtained. Imaging shows multiple stacked air–fluid levels in centrally located dilated loops with little distal gas. Labs show: Sodium 134 m Eq/L Potassium 3.1 m Eq/L Chloride 90 m Eq/L Bicarbonate 33 m Eq/L Blood urea nitrogen 38 mg/d L Creatinine 1.6 mg/d L Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management? In a stable patient with an obstructive pattern, start NPO + nasogastric decompression with aggressive IV fluids and electrolyte repletion; involve surgery early and operate if peritonitis/ischemia develops or no improvement within 24–48 hours.

options.C: Intravenous opioids for analgesia without decompression
options.D: Nasogastric tube decompression with bowel rest

answer_text: Nasogastric tube decompression with bowel rest
explanation: The presentation—vomiting with volume depletion, obstipation, intermittent high-pitched bowel sounds, and an upright film showing stacked air–fluid levels with scant distal gas—indicates a mechanical obstructive pattern. Early priorities are resuscitation and decompression to reduce intraluminal pressure, limit further emesis and aspiration risk, and improve venous outflow in distended loops. Placing a nasogastric tube while keeping the patient NPO offloads accumulated fluid and gas, easing pain and nausea and helping prevent progression to strangulation as the team monitors response. Pathophysiologically, obstruction proximal to a transition point causes sequestration of fluid and electrolytes within the lumen and bowel wall, while repetitive vomiting drives hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis and prerenal azotemia. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: The presentation—vomiting with volume depletion, obstipation, intermittent high-pitched bowel sounds, and an upright film showing stacked air–fluid levels with scant distal gas—indicates a mechanical obstructive pattern. Early priorities are resuscitation and decompression to reduce intraluminal pressure.

stem: A 25-year-old man comes to the clinic due to progressive joint pain and stiffness over the past 5 months. He reports morning stiffness in his hands that gradually improves with use throughout the day. He also notes deformity and reduced mobility in several fingers, especially at the distal interphalangeal joints. He has experienced intermittent episodes of bloody diarrhea over the past year but has not yet undergone formal evaluation. He takes no medications and has no family history of autoimmune conditions. On examination, there is tenderness and reduced range of motion in multiple DIP joints. His bowel sounds are normal, and no rash or oral ulcers are noted. A hand radiograph is shown below. Psoriatic arthritis can present before skin findings, especially with DIP involvement and bowel symptoms. Pencil-in-cup deformities on imaging are classic and highly specific for this diagnosis. Oral methotrexate is the first-line treatment for systemic joint involvement in psoriatic disease. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This patient presents with chronic joint pain, particularly involving the distal interphalangeal (DIP) joints, accompanied by a history of bloody diarrhea, which strongly supports a diagnosis of psoriatic arthritis. The morning stiffness that improves with activity, joint deformities, and DIP involvement are consistent with inflammatory arthritis. The X-ray provided shows the classic “pencil-in-cup” deformities, representing erosive changes at the DIP joints — a hallmark radiographic feature of psoriatic arthritis. The association with bloody bowel movements highlights the HLA-B27 link, as psoriatic arthritis commonly coexists with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD). stem: A 10-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his parents due to fever, abdominal swelling, and decreased appetite over the past 24 hours. He has a known history of steroid-responsive nephrotic syndrome and has been in partial remission for the past 3 months. His medications include prednisone and enalapril. On examination, his temperature is 102.1°F (38.9°C), blood pressure is 100/60 mm Hg, pulse is 115/min, and respiratory rate is 24/min. He appears fatigued. The abdomen is distended with a fluid wave, and there is diffuse tenderness to palpation without rebound or guarding. Bowel sounds are present. Laboratory tests show leukocytosis. An urgent diagnostic paracentesis is performed, revealing turbid fluid with a neutrophil count of 400/mm³ and gram-negative rods on Gram stain. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient? Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis is an important complication of nephrotic syndrome due to impaired humoral immunity and low-protein ascitic fluid. Diagnosis is made via paracentesis with neutrophil count ≥250/mm³, and treatment involves third-generation cephalosporins.
explanation: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) is a bacterial infection of ascitic fluid that occurs in the absence of an evident intra-abdominal source, and it most commonly affects patients with chronic ascites. Although classically seen in adults with cirrhosis, children with nephrotic syndrome are also at increased risk due to the loss of immunoglobulins (especially Ig G) and complement factors in the urine, as well as impaired opsonization of bacteria in ascitic fluid. SBP is often caused by translocation of gut flora, especially gram-negative bacilli like E. coli and Klebsiella pneumoniae. stem: A 48-year-old man comes to the emergency department because he is vomiting blood. He reports that over the past 2 days he has had increasing episodes of nausea, a feeling of abdominal fullness, and dark stools. Six months ago, he was hospitalized for 3 months due to severe alcohol-induced pancreatitis. His recovery was complicated by the development of a large pancreatic pseudocyst in the tail of the pancreas that required surgical drainage. Since that time, he has noticed intermittent upper abdominal discomfort, early satiety, and occasional bloating after meals. He has not had fevers, chills, or unintentional weight loss. On examination, the patient appears pale but is in no acute distress. His vital signs are stable. Abdominal examination reveals mild tenderness to palpation in the left upper quadrant and a firm mass palpable below the left costal margin. There is no fluid wave or shifting dullness. Laboratory studies show hemoglobin concentration of 9.4 g/d L. Upper endoscopy reveals isolated gastric varices without evidence of esophageal varices. Which of the following vessels is most likely thrombosed? Splenic vein thrombosis is a complication of chronic pancreatitis, particularly with pancreatic tail involvement, and presents with isolated gastric varices and splenomegaly. The pathophysiology involves venous obstruction, leading to backflow into the short gastric veins.
explanation: Splenic vein thrombosis is a well-recognized complication of chronic pancreatitis, particularly when the inflammation or a pseudocyst involves the pancreatic tail, which lies in close proximity to the splenic vein as it courses posteriorly to the pancreas. Repeated bouts of inflammation or mechanical compression from the pseudocyst can cause endothelial injury, turbulent flow, and eventual venous thrombosis. The splenic vein receives venous drainage from the spleen, short gastric veins, and the left gastroepiploic vein before uniting with the superior mesenteric vein to form the portal vein. When the splenic vein is obstructed, blood flow from the short gastric veins is blocked, leading to localized portal hypertension in the gastric fundus. stem: A 44-year-old man comes to the clinic because of persistent upper abdominal fullness and discomfort for the past 3 weeks. He says the pain is dull, worse when leaning forward, and associated with early satiety. He has a history of multiple emergency visits for severe abdominal pain and vomiting, most recently about 6 weeks ago, which was attributed to “inflammation of the pancreas.” Since then, his symptoms improved but never completely resolved. He has no history of gallstones but admits to frequent alcohol use. He has unintentionally lost 5 kg (11 lb) and denies fevers, jaundice, hematemesis, or changes in bowel movements. On examination, he appears tired but not acutely ill. Temperature is 37.1°C (98.8°F), pulse is 92/min, blood pressure is 128/76 mm Hg, and BMI is 22 kg/m². The abdomen is soft but mildly distended, with a tender, palpable epigastric mass. Laboratory values show normal amylase and lipase concentrations. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis? A tender, palpable epigastric mass appearing weeks after acute pancreatitis is most consistent with a pancreatic pseudocyst—a fibrous-walled, enzyme-rich fluid collection that can compress nearby structures.
explanation: The timing and presentation are classic: several weeks after an acute pancreatitis episode, the patient develops persistent epigastric pain with a palpable, tender epigastric mass, early satiety from extrinsic gastric compression, and weight loss. Enzymes often normalize once the acute attack resolves, so normal amylase/lipase at this stage does not exclude a pancreatic complication. Alcohol use is a major risk factor, and the collection typically lies in the lesser sac adjacent to the pancreas, explaining the mid-epigastric fullness and discomfort that worsens when leaning forward. A pancreatic pseudocyst forms when enzyme-rich fluid, necrotic debris, and blood are walled off by granulation and fibrous tissue without a true epithelial lining. stem: A 65-year-old woman comes to the physician because of increasing fatigue and lightheadedness over the past 4 months. She denies abdominal pain, diarrhea, or constipation. She has no noticeable blood in her stool. Physical examination is unremarkable. Laboratory studies show microcytic anemia. Colon adenocarcinoma presents differently depending on location: right-sided (ascending) lesions cause iron deficiency anemia from occult bleeding, while left-sided (descending and sigmoid) lesions cause obstruction and hematochezia. In older adults, unexplained microcytic anemia strongly suggests an ascending colon tumor. Which of the following is the most likely location of the patient's primary tumor?
explanation: Right-sided (proximal) colon cancers often present with iron deficiency anemia due to slow, chronic occult blood loss. These lesions tend to be exophytic and bleed rather than obstruct, which explains why this patient has fatigue, pallor, and microcytic hypochromic anemia without a change in bowel habits or obstructive symptoms. In older adults, especially postmenopausal women, unexplained iron deficiency anemia should raise immediate concern for gastrointestinal malignancy, particularly right-sided colon cancer. In contrast, left-sided (distal) colon cancers more commonly cause obstructive symptoms, such as altered bowel habits, constipation, and crampy abdominal pain, because they tend to form annular, circumferential lesions that narrow the lumen. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: Right-sided (proximal) colon cancers often present with iron deficiency anemia due to slow, chronic occult blood loss. These lesions tend to be exophytic and bleed rather than obstruct, which explains why this patient has fatigue, pallor.

stem: A 54-year-old woman comes to the physician due to fatigue and persistent itching, especially over her arms and trunk. She reports mild difficulty with balance and occasionally bumps into furniture at home. Her past medical history includes Hashimoto thyroiditis. She denies alcohol use. Examination shows scleral icterus and excoriations on the skin. Neurologic examination reveals decreased vibratory sensation in the lower extremities and a mildly positive Romberg sign. Laboratory studies show: Hemoglobin 13.6 g/d L MCV 88 f L Total bilirubin 6.2 mg/d L Alkaline phosphatase 620 U/L ALT 45 U/L AST 39 U/L Antimitochondrial antibody titer 1:160 (normal <1:40) Which of the following vitamin deficiencies is most likely responsible for this patient's neurologic symptoms? Primary biliary cholangitis can lead to fat-soluble vitamin deficiencies due to cholestasis and impaired bile acid–mediated absorption. Vitamin E deficiency causes neurologic dysfunction, including impaired balance and posterior column findings. Always evaluate for subtle neurologic signs and vitamin deficiencies in patients with chronic cholestatic liver disease.
explanation: This patient likely has primary biliary cholangitis (PBC), as suggested by her cholestatic lab pattern (elevated ALP and bilirubin), positive anti-mitochondrial antibodies, and clinical features of pruritus, scleral icterus, and fatigue. PBC is a chronic autoimmune condition that leads to destruction of intrahepatic bile ducts. USMLE loves to mention autoimmune disease in the patient or family member (Hashimoto thyroiditis in this patient). Diagnosis is confirmed by liver biopsy. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.

stem: A 68-year-old man is evaluated in the emergency department for painless jaundice that began 4 days ago. He reports dark urine, pale stools, and an 8-lb (3.6-kg) unintentional weight loss over the past month but denies fever, abdominal pain, nausea, or vomiting. His medical history is notable for type 2 diabetes mellitus and an uncomplicated cholecystectomy performed 7 years ago. Medications include metformin and atorvastatin, unchanged for several years. He has a 45-pack-year smoking history. Temperature is 36.7 °C (98.0 °F), pulse 78/min, and blood pressure 130/76 mm Hg. Scleral icterus is present. There is no hepatosplenomegaly and no abdominal tenderness. Laboratory studies show: Total bilirubin 15 mg/d L (direct 12 mg/d L) ALP 620 U/L AST 48 U/L ALT 52 U/L Amylase & Lipase Normal Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis? Pancreatic head cancer causes painless obstructive jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, and weight loss. A history of smoking, diabetes, and remote cholecystectomy supports the diagnosis. The correct next step is CT abdomen, and management depends on tumor location and resectability.
explanation: Pancreatic adenocarcinoma arising in the head of the pancreas classically impinges on the distal common bile duct, producing an obstructive pattern of jaundice with elevated alkaline phosphatase and direct (conjugated) bilirubin. Patients are typically smokers or have other risk factors (e.g., long-standing diabetes) and present with painless jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, and unexplained weight loss. Because the biliary tree is blocked distal to the gallbladder, bile backs up and distends the gallbladder; on exam this can manifest as Courvoisier sign (a painless, palpable gallbladder)—which is pancreatic cancer until proven otherwise on USMLE. Pancreatic enzymes are normal in pancreatic cancer (HY). stem: A 2-month-old boy is brought to the clinic due to frequent episodes of milk regurgitation. His parents report that he spits up two to three times daily during or shortly after feeding. He is otherwise healthy, with normal growth and development, and feeds eagerly. There is no vomiting that is forceful, bilious, or associated with distress. He has no history of fever, choking during feeds, or bloody stools. Vital signs are normal, and physical examination is unremarkable. What is the most likely underlying cause of this patient’s condition? Pediatric GERD presents as non-bilious, non-forceful milk regurgitation several times a day. The cause is immature LES, and treatment begins with thickened feeds and positional therapy.
explanation: This infant presents with classic pediatric gastroesophageal reflux (GERD), which occurs due to an immature lower esophageal sphincter (LES) that allows gastric contents to reflux into the esophagus. Pediatric GERD typically presents during the first few months of life with frequent, non-forceful, non-bilious milk regurgitation during or after feeds. Unlike adult GERD, it is not driven by acid overproduction, but by developmental immaturity of LES tone. On USMLE, students often overlook GERD as a pediatric diagnosis, but it is a common and benign finding in healthy infants. stem: A 42-year-old man is brought to the emergency department due to progressive behavioral changes, frequent falls, and difficulty walking. He has a long history of alcohol use disorder and poor nutritional intake. On examination, he is disoriented, has difficulty with horizontal eye movements, and is unable to stand without support. He dies a few days later from complications of aspiration pneumonia. A coronal brain section at autopsy is shown below. Which of the following best explains this patient's neurological findings? Wernicke encephalopathy is caused by thiamine deficiency and presents with confusion, ataxia, and Ophthalmoplegia. The most characteristic finding on pathology is mammillary body atrophy. Always administer thiamine before glucose in alcoholics or malnourished patients to prevent or reverse this life-threatening condition.

options.E: Wallerian degeneration of the corticospinal tract

explanation: This patient most likely had Wernicke encephalopathy caused by thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency, a common complication of chronic alcohol use and malnutrition. On USMLE, Wernicke encephalopathy presents as “A COW” = Ataxia/Anterograde amnesia, Confusion, Ophthalmoplegia, Wernicke. If left untreated, the condition can progress to Korsakoff syndrome, which is characterized by retrograde amnesia that results in confabulation (filling in memory gaps with made-up stories). So, “A COW + confabulations” = Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: This patient most likely had Wernicke encephalopathy caused by thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency, a common complication of chronic alcohol use and malnutrition. On USMLE, Wernicke encephalopathy presents as “A COW” = Ataxia/Anterograde amnesia, Confusion, Ophthalmoplegia, Wernicke.

stem: A 38-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressive difficulty swallowing. She reports that both solids and liquids get “stuck” in her chest, especially when she eats quickly. She sometimes has to drink water to "push food down." She denies weight loss, chest pain, or heartburn, but mentions a sensation of food regurgitating into her mouth when she lies down. She has no significant past medical history. On physical examination, vital signs are within normal limits, and the exam is unremarkable. A barium swallow (shown) reveals a dilated esophagus with a narrowed distal segment. Esophageal manometry demonstrates increased lower esophageal sphincter (LES) resting pressure and incomplete LES relaxation with swallowing. There is also absence of normal peristaltic waves in the distal esophagus. Upper endoscopy shows no masses or strictures. Achalasia is caused by degeneration of ganglion cells within the myenteric plexus, leading to impaired relaxation of the lower esophageal sphincter and absent peristalsis. Patients present with progressive dysphagia to both solids and liquids, regurgitation, and a "bird’s beak" sign on barium swallow. Manometry shows increased LES pressure and aperistalsis. Which of the following best describes the underlying pathology in this patient?

options.A: Degeneration of ganglion cells within the myenteric plexus
options.B: Esophageal mucosal erosion with columnar metaplasia
options.C: Fibrotic narrowing of the distal esophagus due to chronic acidexposure
options.D: Infiltration of the esophageal wall with eosinophils
options.E: Sclerotic thickening of esophageal smooth muscle due toautoimmune damage

answer_text: Degeneration of ganglion cells within the myenteric plexus
explanation: This patient has progressive dysphagia to both solids and liquids, along with manometry findings showing impaired LES relaxation and absent peristalsis — a classic presentation of achalasia. The underlying cause of primary achalasia is the degeneration of ganglion cells in the myenteric (Auerbach) plexus, especially the inhibitory neurons that release nitric oxide and vasoactive intestinal peptide (VIP). These neurotransmitters are essential for coordinating esophageal peristalsis and relaxing the LES during swallowing. When these ganglion cells are lost, the LES remains tonically contracted and fails to relax appropriately, causing functional obstruction at the gastroesophageal junction. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: This patient has progressive dysphagia to both solids and liquids, along with manometry findings showing impaired LES relaxation and absent peristalsis — a classic presentation of achalasia. The underlying cause of primary achalasia is the degeneration.

stem: A 72-year-old man comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of progressive difficulty swallowing. He describes the sensation that both solid foods and liquids seem to slow down behind the lower part of his breast bone shortly after eating. He sometimes regurgitates undigested food but denies weight loss, chest pain, or heartburn. He does not smoke or drink alcohol. He has no prior upper gastrointestinal procedures and takes only a statin for hyperlipidemia. His physical examination is unremarkable. A barium swallow is shown below. Achalasia is a motility disorder due to impaired LES relaxation and loss of esophageal peristalsis, resulting in dysphagia to both solids and liquids. It is best described as esophageal sphincter dysfunction and is confirmed by manometry and supported by classic barium imaging. Treatment targets LES pressure reduction to relieve symptoms and prevent complications. What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?
explanation: This patient’s symptoms are most likely caused by esophageal sphincter dysfunction, specifically achalasia. Achalasia is an esophageal motility disorder caused by impaired neuromuscular transmission in the esophageal wall, leading to failure of the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) to relax and absence of peristalsis in the esophageal body. The condition results from the destruction of inhibitory neurons in the myenteric (Auerbach) plexus, which decreases nitric oxide–mediated smooth muscle relaxation. The resulting increase in LES pressure and ineffective peristalsis lead to gradual esophageal dilation, food retention, and symptoms such as progressive dysphagia, regurgitation of undigested food, halitosis, and sometimes weight loss. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: This patient’s symptoms are most likely caused by esophageal sphincter dysfunction, specifically achalasia. Achalasia is an esophageal motility disorder caused by impaired neuromuscular transmission in the esophageal wall, leading to failure of the lower esophageal sphincter.

stem: A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of difficulty swallowing solid foods that has been gradually worsening over the past year. She reports intermittent fatigue and recently noticed her tongue feels sore when eating spicy foods. Her diet consists mainly of vegetables and grains, and she avoids red meat due to personal preferences. She denies chest pain, reflux, weight loss, or changes in appetite. On physical exam, she appears pale and has dry, brittle nails that appear spoon-shaped. Cardiac and abdominal exams are unremarkable. Neurological examination is normal. Vitals are: temperature 36.7°C (98.1°F), blood pressure 116/72 mm Hg, pulse 84/min, respiratory rate 16/min, and BMI 22.6 kg/m². Which of the following is the most likely underlying risk factor in this patient?
explanation: Plummer-Vinson syndrome is the triad of IDA, angular cheilitis, and esophageal webs. Koilonychia (spoon-shaped nails) can be seen in severe IDA. The development of esophageal webs—thin mucosal membranes partially obstructing the upper esophagus—is thought to result from chronic mucosal atrophy due to iron deficiency impairing epithelial cell turnover. This can lead to dysphagia, particularly for solids. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: Plummer-Vinson syndrome is the triad of IDA, angular cheilitis, and esophageal webs. Koilonychia (spoon-shaped nails) can be seen in severe IDA.

Esophagogastroduodenoscopy reveals a thin membrane in the upper esophagus. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis? Plummer-Vinson syndrome is characterized by iron deficiency anemia, esophageal webs, and angular cheilitis. Additional signs include pica, koilonychia, and dysphagia to solids. options.C: Iron deficiency anemia due to celiac disease

subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 52-year-old man is admitted with sudden onset hematemesis and signs of hypovolemic shock. He has a background of chronic alcohol use, jaundice, and was previously hospitalized for hepatic encephalopathy and upper GI bleeding. Endoscopic band ligation of his esophageal varices failed to control recurrent bleeding, and nonselective beta-blockers were discontinued due to bradycardia. On exam, he has tense ascites, scleral icterus, and spider angiomata. Laboratory tests show thrombocytopenia, elevated INR, and low albumin. Abdominal ultrasound reveals a shrunken liver, portal vein dilation, and splenomegaly. After initial stabilization and octreotide infusion, the surgical team discusses portosystemic shunt options to decompress the portal system. Which of the following vascular anastomoses is most appropriate to directly reduce portal hypertension and control his refractory variceal bleeding?

options.A: Inferior mesenteric vein and internal iliac vein
options.B: Left gastric vein and azygos vein
options.D: Splenic vein and left renal vein
options.E: Superior mesenteric vein and inferior vena cava

answer_text: Splenic vein and left renal vein
explanation: The distal splenorenal shunt (anastomosis between the splenic vein and left renal vein) is a surgical portosystemic shunt used to decompress the portal circulation in patients with refractory variceal bleeding, especially when endoscopic and pharmacologic therapies fail. This procedure selectively diverts blood from the high-pressure portal system (via the splenic vein) into the systemic venous circulation (left renal vein), thereby reducing pressure in the short gastric and esophageal veins, which are the primary contributors to esophageal varices. This shunt preserves portal flow to the liver, lowering the risk of hepatic encephalopathy compared to non-selective shunts, and is especially indicated in patients with preserved liver function but recurrent variceal bleeding. The pathophysiology of portal hypertension in cirrhosis involves increased resistance to portal flow due to hepatic fibrosis and nodular regeneration, resulting in the formation of portosystemic collateral vessels. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: The distal splenorenal shunt (anastomosis between the splenic vein and left renal vein) is a surgical portosystemic shunt used to decompress the portal circulation in patients with refractory variceal bleeding, especially when endoscopic and pharmacologic therapies.

subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 49-year-old man presents with intermittent rectal bleeding noticed as streaks of blood on toilet paper and in the bowl over the past several weeks. He reports a long history of alcohol use and occasional abdominal fullness, ankle swelling, and easy bruising. Over the last few months, he has developed progressive fatigue and mild shortness of breath with exertion. He denies abdominal pain, diarrhea, or recent travel. On examination, he appears chronically ill, with scleral icterus, spider angiomas on the chest, and mild ascites. His spleen is palpable below the costal margin, and he has bilateral ankle edema. Rectal exam reveals no external masses or fissures but occult blood is detected. Laboratory studies show thrombocytopenia, mild anemia, elevated INR, and elevated liver transaminases. Imaging demonstrates a nodular liver consistent with cirrhosis. Endoscopic evaluation reveals dilated venous structures in the rectum consistent with internal hemorrhoids. Which of the following best explains the underlying mechanism responsible for this patient’s rectal findings?

options.A: Increased blood flow between inferior mesenteric veins and azygos veins
options.B: Increased blood flow between portal vein and inferior vena cava through the paraumbilical veins
options.C: Increased blood flow between splenic vein and short gastric veins
options.D: Increased blood flow between superior mesenteric veins and lumbar veins
options.E: Increased blood flow between superior rectal veins and inferior rectal veins

answer_text: Increased blood flow between superior rectal veins and inferior rectal veins
explanation: Internal hemorrhoids in the setting of portal hypertension reflect congestion of the submucosal plexus above the dentate line where the portal drainage of the superior rectal vein (to the inferior mesenteric → splenic → portal vein) communicates with the systemic drainage of the middle and inferior rectal veins (to the internal iliac → common iliac → IVC). Increased flow and pressure across this portosystemic anastomosis enlarge these cushions, producing painless bright red bleeding and mucosal prolapse. This patient’s cirrhosis stigmata—ascites, splenomegaly with thrombocytopenia, spider angiomas, coagulopathy—identify portal hypertension as the driver of the anorectal venous engorgement. Portal hypertension classically generates dilated collateral beds where portal and systemic circulations meet, explaining the clinical triad of varices, caput medusae, and hemorrhoids/anorectal varices. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: Internal hemorrhoids in the setting of portal hypertension reflect congestion of the submucosal plexus above the dentate line where the portal drainage of the superior rectal vein (to the inferior mesenteric → splenic → portal vein).

subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 39-year-old male presents to the clinic for evaluation of intermittent scleral icterus that began approximately 6 days ago. He reports mild fatigue but denies abdominal pain, pruritus, dark urine, or pale stools. He underwent an uncomplicated laparoscopic cholecystectomy three weeks prior. He has no prior history of liver disease or significant alcohol use. Physical examination reveals mild jaundice but is otherwise unremarkable. Laboratory studies show: total bilirubin 2.6 mg/d L (reference: 0.2–1.2), direct bilirubin 0.2 mg/d L (reference: 0.0–0.3), AST 24 U/L (reference: 10–40), ALT 28 U/L (reference: 7–56), ALP 66 U/L (reference: 44–147), LDH 189 U/L (reference: 120–250). Hemoglobin and reticulocyte counts are normal. He has never noticed similar symptoms before, but his brother has had mild jaundice during periods of illness. Eye examination is shown below: Gilbert syndrome is a benign, inherited condition caused by reduced UGT1A1 activity, leading to intermittent unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia, often triggered by stress. Patients are asymptomatic aside from mild jaundice, with normal liver function and no hemolysis. No treatment is needed, and the prognosis is excellent. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for these findings?
explanation: Gilbert syndrome is a benign inherited disorder caused by decreased activity of the hepatic enzyme UDP-glucuronosyltransferase, responsible for conjugating unconjugated (indirect) bilirubin within hepatocytes. The mutation most commonly involves the UGT1A1 gene, leading to reduced bilirubin conjugation and intermittent episodes of unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia, often triggered by physiological stress such as fasting, illness, or recent surgery. Patients are usually asymptomatic except for mild jaundice, with normal liver function tests and no evidence of hemolysis. The laboratory findings show isolated elevation of total bilirubin with a normal direct (conjugated) bilirubin fraction, normal LDH, and no evidence of anemia or hemolysis. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: Gilbert syndrome is a benign inherited disorder caused by decreased activity of the hepatic enzyme UDP-glucuronosyltransferase, responsible for conjugating unconjugated (indirect) bilirubin within hepatocytes. The mutation most commonly involves the UGT1A1 gene, leading to reduced bilirubin.

subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 66-year-old man presents with progressive fatigue and unintentional weight loss over the past 4 months. He reports early satiety, mild right upper quadrant discomfort, and intermittent constipation. He denies nausea, vomiting, or visible blood in stool. He has a history of type 2 diabetes and hypertension. He does not smoke, drink alcohol, or take any hepatotoxic medications. There is no known history of viral hepatitis or liver disease. He has never undergone cancer screening. On physical exam, the patient appears thin but in no acute distress. Sclerae are anicteric. The abdomen is soft, with a firm, non-tender liver edge palpable 3 cm below the right costal margin. Laboratory studies reveal: AST 51 U/L ALT 44 U/L ALP 189 U/L Total bilirubin 1.4 mg/d L Albumin 3.4 g/d L INR 1.1 AFP is within normal limits. A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the abdomen is performed, followed by a core biopsy of a representative lesion. The liver findings in this patient are similar to those shown in the representative gross pathology specimen below. Hepatocellular Carcinoma – Single Lesion. Choice E: Pancreatic cancer can metastasize to the liver, but typically presents with painless jaundice, Courvoisier sign, or back pain. There’s no mention of biliary obstruction or pancreatic findings on imaging or exam. Additionally, pancreatic cancer does not usually cause widespread hepatic metastases as the initial presentation. Without weight loss and jaundice in the appropriate pattern, this is less likely than colorectal origin. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This patient presents with a constellation of findings suggestive of advanced intrahepatic disease: progressive fatigue, weight loss, early satiety, and a palpable, firm liver edge, all occurring in the absence of chronic liver disease or cirrhosis. The laboratory values reveal mildly elevated liver enzymes and alkaline phosphatase, but normal AFP, which makes a primary hepatic malignancy less likely. Importantly, the clinical context includes no prior cancer screening and chronic intermittent constipation, both of which increase the likelihood of an undiagnosed lower gastrointestinal malignancy. The gross pathology image, showing multiple pale, well-circumscribed nodules scattered throughout the liver, is classic for hepatic metastases, and not a primary liver tumor. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: This patient presents with a constellation of findings suggestive of advanced intrahepatic disease: progressive fatigue, weight loss, early satiety, and a palpable, firm liver edge, all occurring in the absence of chronic liver disease or cirrhosis..

stem: A 24-year-old man presents to clinic one week after undergoing a laparoscopic appendectomy for uncomplicated acute appendicitis. He reports that he has been recovering well overall but noticed that the whites of his eyes appeared yellow over the past two days. He has no abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, or changes in bowel movements. His appetite has improved since surgery, and he is passing gas and tolerating oral intake. He denies fever, chills, dark urine, or pale stools. He has been walking daily and taking only acetaminophen for occasional discomfort. On review of systems, he reports feeling a little fatigued but otherwise well. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications. His family history is notable for his father having “some liver enzyme issue” that was never treated. On exam, he is afebrile and in no distress. Scleral icterus is present, but his skin is not jaundiced. His abdomen is soft and non-tender with healed incisions and no signs of fluid collection. Laboratory testing reveals total bilirubin of 3.1 mg/d L with indirect bilirubin of 2.8 mg/d L, direct bilirubin of 0.3 mg/d L, normal AST, ALT, and alkaline phosphatase, and normal hemoglobin and hematocrit. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis? Gilbert syndrome causes isolated indirect hyperbilirubinemia in the setting of stress, fasting, or illness, with normal liver enzymes and no hemolysis. It commonly presents after surgery or exertion and requires no treatment.
explanation: Gilbert syndrome is a benign inherited condition caused by reduced activity of the hepatic enzyme UDP-glucuronosyltransferase, which is responsible for conjugating bilirubin. When this enzyme is underactive, unconjugated (indirect) bilirubin accumulates in the blood, especially during periods of physiologic stress such as illness, fasting, surgery, or infection. This patient’s development of scleral icterus in the week following appendectomy is classic, as even minor surgical stress can trigger a transient rise in indirect bilirubin in those with Gilbert syndrome. The key laboratory findings include isolated indirect hyperbilirubinemia with otherwise normal liver function tests and no anemia. stem: A 73-year-old man undergoes elective abdominal surgery for resection of an infrarenal abdominal mass discovered incidentally on imaging. He has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia but is otherwise in good health. He has never undergone colonoscopy and denies prior gastrointestinal bleeding, changes in bowel habits, abdominal pain, or unexplained weight loss. Intraoperatively, the surgeon notes multiple small, saclike protrusions of mucosa and submucosa through the muscular layer of the colon wall. These protrusions are most numerous in one segment of the large intestine and are separated by areas of normal mucosa. The serosal surface shows no signs of active inflammation or perforation. The patient’s postoperative course is uneventful, and he remains asymptomatic. Which of the following colonic segments is most commonly affected in this patient? Acquired colonic diverticula most often involve the sigmoid colon due to its high intraluminal pressures. Risk factors include advanced age, low-fiber diet, and constipation. Most patients are asymptomatic, but complications—such as diverticulitis, bleeding, and fistula formation—can be serious and require prompt recognition and appropriate management.
explanation: Acquired colonic diverticula are false diverticula, meaning they consist of mucosa and submucosa herniating through weak points in the muscularis propria, usually at sites where vasa recta penetrate the muscle layer. They are most common in the sigmoid colon, which has the smallest lumen and experiences the highest intraluminal pressures during stool propulsion. The high-pressure environment predisposes this segment to mucosal outpouching, especially with low-fiber diets that produce small, firm stools and require greater colonic segmentation forces. stem: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department because of intermittent episodes of painless rectal bleeding over the past two days. His parents report that the stools appear maroon-colored and have occurred without associated fever, vomiting, or diarrhea. The child has normal appetite and activity between episodes. On physical exam, he is well-appearing, with a soft, non-tender abdomen and no hepatosplenomegaly. His hemoglobin is 10.8 g/d L, and stool is positive for occult blood. Colonoscopy is normal. A technetium-99m pertechnetate scan shows increased uptake in the right lower quadrant. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's anemia? Meckel diverticulum can contain gastric and pancreatic heterotopic tissue, leading to bleeding and iron deficiency anemia. It need not occur in kids.

options.A: Angiodysplasia of the terminal ileum causing vascular ectasia and bleeding
options.B: Autoimmune-mediated inflammation of colonic crypts leading to ulceration
options.C: Ectopic acid production from gastric mucosa within a true diverticulum
options.D: Intussusception with ischemic mucosal necrosis and bloody stools
options.E: Lymphoid hyperplasia triggering appendiceal obstruction andinflammation

answer_text: Ectopic acid production from gastric mucosa within a true diverticulum
explanation: Meckel diverticulum is a true diverticulum (involves all layers of bowel - mucosa, submucosa, and muscularis propria) that will present as an outpouching at or around the terminal ileum. There can be heterotopic (ectopic) gastric and pancreatic tissue within the diverticulum that secretes acid/enzymes that leads to bleeding and iron defiency anemia. This is exceedingly HY for USMLE. A Meckel scan, or Tc99 scan, can be used to diagnose, which shows localization to the terminal ileum. stem: A 17-year-old boy presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of intermittent abdominal pain and diarrhea. He describes the abdominal pain as crampy and located in the right lower quadrant. His stools are loose, occur three to five times daily, and occasionally contain mucus but no visible blood. Over the past two months, he has unintentionally lost 5 kg (11 lb) and reports increased fatigue. He also complains of occasional low-grade fevers and joint stiffness in the morning that improves throughout the day. On physical exam, his temperature is 37.8°C (100.0°F), pulse is 92/min, and blood pressure is 110/68 mm Hg. The abdomen is tender to palpation in the right lower quadrant with no rebound or guarding. Perianal exam reveals a skin tag and small fissure. Lab tests reveal hemoglobin 10.8 g/d L, mean corpuscular volume 74 f L, and C-reactive protein 35 mg/L. Colonoscopy shows areas of patchy inflammation with deep linear ulcerations and cobblestone appearance in the terminal ileum and ascending colon. Biopsies are obtained. Which of the following histologic findings is most characteristic of this condition? Crohn disease is characterized by transmural inflammation with possible non-caseating granulomas, patchy involvement, and complications like fistulae or perianal disease.

options.A: Accumulation of intraepithelial lymphocytes and villous blunting
options.B: Crypt abscesses and continuous mucosal inflammation
options.C: Hypertrophy of the muscularis mucosae and fecalomas

explanation: This patient presents with clinical, endoscopic, and laboratory features consistent with Crohn disease. Crohn disease is a chronic inflammatory bowel disease that can affect any part of the gastrointestinal tract, most commonly the terminal ileum. It is characterized by transmural inflammation, which can lead to complications such as strictures, fistulae, and abscesses. A key histologic hallmark of Crohn disease is non-caseating granulomatous inflammation that penetrates through all layers of the bowel wall. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: This patient presents with clinical, endoscopic, and laboratory features consistent with Crohn disease. Crohn disease is a chronic inflammatory bowel disease that can affect any part of the gastrointestinal tract, most commonly the terminal ileum.

stem: A 62-year-old man presents to the emergency department with 3 days of worsening abdominal pain and low-grade fever. He describes the pain as a constant, dull ache localized to the lower left quadrant of his abdomen. He also reports decreased appetite, nausea, and occasional loose stools over the past 2 days. He denies vomiting, hematochezia, or melena. He has had similar, less intense episodes in the past that resolved without treatment, but this time the pain has been more severe and persistent. His medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension. He does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. He had a normal colonoscopy 5 years ago. On exam, his temperature is 38.2°C (100.8°F), blood pressure is 128/74 mm Hg, and pulse is 94/min. Abdominal exam reveals tenderness to palpation in the left lower quadrant without rebound or guarding. Bowel sounds are slightly decreased. Rectal exam is unremarkable. Laboratory studies show leukocytosis. A urinalysis is negative for infection or hematuria. An abdominal CT scan with contrast demonstrates pericolic fat stranding and localized bowel wall thickening of the sigmoid colon without abscess or free air. What is the most likely diagnosis? Diverticulitis is a common cause of LLQ pain and fever in older adults. Diagnosis is confirmed by CT imaging, and uncomplicated cases are typically managed conservatively with antibiotics and bowel rest.
explanation: This patient presents with classic features of acute diverticulitis, which is inflammation and infection of a colonic diverticulum, most commonly occurring in the sigmoid colon. The typical presentation includes left lower quadrant (LLQ) pain, fever, and leukocytosis. Additional symptoms may include bowel habit changes (e.g., diarrhea or constipation), anorexia, and nausea. The LLQ predominance reflects the higher prevalence of sigmoid diverticulosis in Western populations, where low-fiber diets are common. stem: A 19-year-old college student presents to urgent care with worsening abdominal discomfort that began 36 hours ago. He states that the pain initially started as a dull ache near his umbilicus and was accompanied by nausea and a sense of bloating. He attributed the symptoms to something he ate at the dining hall and tried resting, but the pain has steadily worsened. Over the past 12 hours, the discomfort has become sharper and now feels localized to the right lower quadrant. He also notes loss of appetite and one episode of vomiting earlier this morning. He denies diarrhea, constipation, or recent travel. He has not had similar symptoms in the past and has no known medical conditions. He is sexually active with one partner and uses condoms inconsistently. He denies urinary symptoms or penile discharge. On physical exam, he appears slightly uncomfortable and is lying still on the examination table. His abdomen is soft but tender in the right lower quadrant, with localized guarding. Rebound tenderness is noted in the same area, and there is mild voluntary rigidity. Rovsing sign is positive, and he reports increased pain with extension of the right hip. Bowel sounds are present. No costovertebral angle tenderness is noted. Genital exam is normal. The remainder of the physical exam is unremarkable. Which of the following is the underlying mechanism responsible for the shift in the location of this patient’s pain? The shift in abdominal pain from vague periumbilical to sharp right lower quadrant is due to inflammation of the parietal peritoneum, which triggers somatic sensory pathways. This transition is characteristic of appendicitis and reflects disease progression requiring surgical evaluation.
explanation: This patient presents with a classic progression of appendiceal inflammation, which characteristically begins as vague periumbilical pain and then localizes to the right lower quadrant as inflammation progresses. The initial diffuse pain is due to visceral afferent fibers associated with the midgut, which relay poorly localized signals to the T10 dermatome, resulting in midline discomfort. As the disease worsens and the serosal surface of the appendix begins to irritate the adjacent parietal peritoneum, the pain becomes sharp, well-localized, and somatic. This shift in pain location is a hallmark feature of peritonitis and represents involvement of the somatic nerves (e.g., intercostal nerves) that innervate the parietal peritoneum. bottom_line: This patient presents with a classic progression of appendiceal inflammation, which characteristically begins as vague periumbilical pain and then localizes to the right lower quadrant as inflammation progresses. The initial diffuse pain is due to visceral.

subject: Gastroenterology
stem: A 71-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after passing a large amount of bright red blood in his stool earlier this morning. He says he woke up feeling well, had breakfast, and then experienced an urgent need to defecate, producing a large volume of red blood mixed with small clots. He denies abdominal pain, dizziness, fever, or recent changes in bowel habits. This has never happened before. The patient has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia, as well as occasional constipation. His medications include lisinopril and atorvastatin. He has no history of gastrointestinal disease on prior colonoscopy. He does not smoke, drinks alcohol occasionally, and lives independently with his spouse. On arrival, he appears alert and in no acute distress. Temperature is 36.9°C (98.4°F), blood pressure is 124/76 mm Hg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 14/min. Cardiac and pulmonary examinations are normal. Abdominal examination reveals a soft, nondistended abdomen without tenderness, guarding, or palpable masses. Digital rectal examination shows bright red blood but no palpable masses or fissures. Initial laboratory studies show hemoglobin 11.2 g/d L, mean corpuscular volume 90 f L, platelets 230,000/mm³, and normal coagulation studies. Basic metabolic panel is normal. Intravenous fluids are started, and the patient remains hemodynamically stable. Colonoscopy is arranged and performed within 12 hours. Endoscopic epinephrine injection and cauterization are performed with successful hemostasis. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient’s presentation? Diverticular bleeding is a leading cause of acute painless hematochezia in older adults, resulting from rupture of the vasa recta within a diverticulum. Colonoscopy during active bleeding confirms the diagnosis and allows for targeted therapy.

options.A: Arteriolar rupture within a colonic mucosal outpouching
options.B: Autoimmune-mediated crypt destruction with continuous mucosal ulceration
options.C: Inflammation of diverticular sac with localized perforation
options.D: Ischemic necrosis of colonic mucosa from arterial thromboembolism
options.E: Neoplastic invasion of colonic mucosal vessels

answer_text: Arteriolar rupture within a colonic mucosal outpouching
explanation: Diverticulosis is characterized by sac-like protrusions of the mucosa and submucosa through the muscularis propria of the colon, most commonly at points of weakness where the vasa recta penetrate the muscular wall. These protrusions, called false diverticula, lack a muscular layer, which makes the penetrating vessels more exposed to injury over time. In diverticular bleeding, chronic mechanical stress and focal mucosal erosion weaken the arterial wall of the vasa recta within the diverticulum. Eventually, the thin-walled artery ruptures directly into the colonic lumen, producing acute, brisk arterial bleeding. The other options are less likely because they describe alternative diseases, mechanisms, anatomic sites, or complications that do not match the patient’s age, symptom pattern, laboratory findings, imaging, or operative context.
bottom_line: Diverticulosis is characterized by sac-like protrusions of the mucosa and submucosa through the muscularis propria of the colon, most commonly at points of weakness where the vasa recta penetrate the muscular wall. These protrusions, called false.

stem: A 58-year-old man presents with progressive weight loss and early satiety over the past 2 months. He reports occasional bloating and mild epigastric discomfort after meals. Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy with biopsy reveals the histologic section shown, with the area labeled Y indicating the affected cell type. Which of the following is most likely to be decreased in this patient?

options.B: Bicarbonate secretion from surface mucous neck (foveolar) cells

explanation: The cell labeled Y is a chief cell, found in the deeper portion of the gastric glands of the fundus and body. Chief cells are responsible for secreting pepsinogen, the inactive zymogen form of pepsin, which is activated to pepsin in the acidic environment of the stomach. Pepsin initiates protein digestion by breaking down peptide bonds. Histologically, chief cells have basophilic (purple) cytoplasm on H&E staining because they contain abundant rough endoplasmic reticulum for protein synthesis.
